# **1\. User Requirements Definition** {#1.-user-requirements-definition}

# ---

## **1.1. Context Diagram** {#1.1.-context-diagram}

**![][image1]**  
The context diagram provides a high-level overview of the Music Streaming System and its interactions with external entities. This diagram illustrates the system as a single process and identifies the various external actors that exchange information with it.

**System Boundary**  
The central component in the diagram is the Music Streaming System, which represents the software system to be developed. This system interacts with multiple external entities, each serving a distinct role in the music streaming ecosystem.

**External Entities and Interactions**  
Artists: They interact with the system by providing permanent premium profiles, which include their music content and associated metadata.  
Recording Labels: Supply music content and may also receive listener analytics to evaluate performance and engagement metrics.  
Marketing System: Consumes listener analytics from the system to support targeted advertising and promotional strategies.  
Customer Support System: Accesses specific listener metadata to provide personalized assistance and resolve user issues.  
Payment Gateway: Handles payment services and receives specific listener metadata required for transaction processing and verification.  
Guests & Listeners: Are the primary end-users who consume the music streaming services and generate metadata based on their activity (e.g., preferences, listening history).

**Data Flow Summary**  
The system provides music streaming services to users, based on content provided by artists and recording labels.  
Listener metadata is collected during system use and shared with the payment gateway, customer support, and marketing systems.  
Analytics are generated and sent to both marketing systems and recording labels for insights and strategy formulation.  
This diagram helps clarify the key inputs and outputs of the system, the stakeholders involved, and how the system integrates within a broader operational environment. It serves as a foundational view for understanding system requirements and guiding further design.

## 

## **1.2. User Epics and User Stories** {#1.2.-user-epics-and-user-stories}

User epics and user stories are used to capture system requirements from the perspective of end users and stakeholders. These narratives help in understanding the goals, expectations, and workflows that the system must support. They form the foundation for functional requirements and system design decisions.

**User Epics**  
User epics represent high-level goals or features of the system that deliver value to users. Each epic is typically broken down into several user stories. The more detailed user stories describe specific functionalities or interactions. They will be mapped with the corresponding use case, serving as a foundation for the use case diagram and use case description.

The detailed Epics and Stories, along with the mapped UC is defined as follow:

**Epic 1: Music Interaction**  
As a user, I want to explore and interact with songs and playlists so that I can enjoy and manage my music experience.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to view song details so that I can learn more about the song (UC-1).  
As a user, I want to listen to a song so that I can enjoy my music (UC-4).  
As a user, I want to listen to a playlist so that I can enjoy curated songs (UC-5).  
As a user, I want to add/remove songs to/from my favourites so that I can access them easily later (UC-6, UC-7).  
As a user, I want to add/remove playlists to/from my favourites for quick access (UC-8, UC-9).  
As a user, I want to download/remove songs and playlists for offline listening (UC-24–UC-27).

**Epic 2: Playlist Management**  
As a user, I want to manage my playlists so that I can curate music collections that suit my preferences.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to create a playlist so that I can group songs I like (UC-28).  
As a user, I want to update a playlist so that I can modify its content (UC-29).  
As a user, I want to delete a playlist when I no longer need it (UC-30).  
As a user, I want to change playlist access settings to control who can view it (UC-31).  
As a user, I want to view playlist details to understand its contents (UC-2).

**Epic 3: Social Profiles**  
As a user, I want to interact with user profiles so that I can connect with and follow other users.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to view a user profile so that I can learn more about them (UC-3).  
As a user, I want to add/remove user profiles to/from favourites so I can follow people I like (UC-10, UC-11).  
As a user, I want to share a profile with others for social engagement (UC-23).

**Epic 4: Discovery and Search**  
As a user, I want to discover and search content so that I can find songs, playlists, and profiles that interest me.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to search for songs so that I can find music I want to hear (UC-12).  
As a user, I want to search for playlists to explore new collections (UC-13).  
As a user, I want to search for user profiles to follow people or friends (UC-14).  
As a user, I want to view trending songs, playlists, and profiles to stay updated (UC-15–UC-17).  
As a user, I want to view personalized recommendations to discover content I might like (UC-18–UC-20).

**Epic 5: Sharing and Engagement**  
As a user, I want to share content so that I can recommend songs, playlists, or profiles to others.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to share a song with others (UC-21).  
As a user, I want to share a playlist with others (UC-22).

**Epic 6: Account and Access Management**

As a user, I want to manage my account and subscription so that I can control my access and preferences.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to sign up for an account to access the platform (UC-32).  
As a user, I want to sign in/out to manage session access (UC-33, UC-34).  
As a user, I want to recover my account if I lose access (UC-35).  
As a user, I want to delete my account when I no longer want to use the service (UC-36).  
As a user, I want to change my settings to personalize my account (UC-37).

**Epic 7: Subscription Management**  
As a user, I want to manage my subscription so that I can access premium features and control billing.

User Stories:  
As a user, I want to view my subscription details to track my plan and billing (UC-38).  
As a user, I want to activate, cancel, or renew my subscription depending on my needs (UC-39–UC-41).

## **1.3. Use Case Diagram** {#1.3.-use-case-diagram}

**![][image2]**  
Use cases that concern the primary actors are defined here. The Premium Listener extends the Basic Listener, having access to all of the basic listener’s use cases. Other actors not related to the users are not specified here. Refer to the Context Diagram.

##

UC-9: Remove Playlist from Favourites

UC-11: Remove Profile from Favourites

UC-13: Search Playlists

UC-16: View Trending Playlists

UC-19: View Recommended Playlists

UC-22: Share Playlist

UC-25: Remove Song from Downloads  
UC-26: Add Playlist to Downloads  
UC-27: Remove Playlist from Downloads

UC-29: Update Playlist  
UC-30: Delete Playlist  
UC-31: Set Playlist Access Level

UC-32: Sign Up  
UC-33: Sign In  
UC-34: Sign Out  
UC-35: Recover Account  
UC-36: Delete Account

UC-38: View Subscription Details  
UC-39: Activate Subscription  
UC-40: Cancel Subscription  
UC-41: Renew Subscription

### **1.4.2. Detailed use cases description** {#1.4.2.-detailed-use-cases-description}

**UC-1: View Song Details**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener selects a song from a browsing view, to access metadata. This includes the song title, duration, artist name, album name, release date, genres, lyrics (if available), view count, favourites count and associated album art.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet or the song has been downloaded.  
PRE-2: The song exists in the music database.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system presents the complete song details on the user’s device.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener has the options to view the owner's profile (UC-3), view the playlist associated (UC-2), play the song (UC-4), add/remove song to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), return to the previous screen or return to home screen.    
POST-4: Premium Listener can also add/remove songs to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) and download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener chooses the info button of a song from: search view, home view, playlist view, profile view.  
2\. The detailed song page is displayed, showing metadata.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The listener goes to a link of the song from a third party.  
2\. The detailed song page is displayed, showing metadata.

Exception Flow 1:  
1\. If the song has been removed or is unavailable, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or return to the homescreen.

**UC-2: View Playlist Details**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener selects a playlist from any accessible view to examine its details. This includes the playlist title, description (if any), total song count, creation date, playlist owner’s name, and a list of all songs in the playlist with their basic metadata (title, artist, duration, album).

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet or the playlist has been downloaded.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the playlist database and is public. Alternatively, the listener is a Premium Listener and the owner of the playlist.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system presents the complete playlist details on the user’s device.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener has the options to view the playlist owner’s profile details (UC-3), play the entire playlist (UC-5), favourite the playlist (UC-8/UC-9), add/remove individual songs to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), go to any song’s detail page (UC-1), return to the previous screen or return to the homescreen.    
POST-4: Premium Listeners can set the playlist access level if they are the owner (UC-31). Additionally, they can update/delete the playlist (UC-29/UC-30).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener chooses a playlist from: home view, search results, profile view, or their library.  
2\. The detailed playlist page is displayed, showing all metadata and song list.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The listener opens a link to the playlist from a third party.  
2\. The detailed playlist page is displayed, showing all metadata and song list.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist is deleted, private, or is unavailable, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or return to the homescreen.

**UC-3: View Profile Details**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener selects a profile (their own or another user’s) to view details. This includes profile name, profile picture, bio (if available), favourites count, public playlists

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The Listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The profile exists in the system and is public. Alternatively, the listener is a Premium Listener and the profile is the listener's own.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays the profile details on the user’s device.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can follow/unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), view a song details (UC-1), view a playlist details (UC-2), return to the previous screen or return to the homescreen.    
POST-4: If the listener is a Premium listener and the profile is the listener’s own, the listener can update their profile (UC-37).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener selects a profile from: a song page, a playlist, search results, the follower/following list of another profile.  
2\. The detailed profile page is displayed, showing all available metadata.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The listener opens a shared profile link from a third party.  
2\. The detailed profile page is displayed, showing all available metadata.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the profile has been deleted, private, or is unavailable, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or return to the homescreen.

**UC-4: Listen to Song**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener selects a song to begin playback. The system streams the audio or plays it from a downloaded copy, and allows interactive playback controls such as pause, resume, seek, skip, repeat, shuffle, and volume adjustment during listening.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet, or the song has been downloaded.  
PRE-2: The song exists in the music database and is available for playback.  
PRE-3: The listener has playback permissions (e.g., not restricted due to region or content lock).

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The song is being played on the user’s device.  
POST-2: A play event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can view the song’s details (UC-1), view its playlist (UC-2), view its artist’s profile (UC-3).    
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove song to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29). They can also download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener selects a song from song details view, search view, playlist view, profile view, or home view.  
2\. The system begins playback immediately.  
3\. The listener interacts with the playback interface, which includes options to: Pause/resume playback, Seek forward/backward within the track, Toggle repeat (track or none), Toggle shuffle (if part of a larger queue), Adjust volume, View playback progress and duration, Toggle shuffle

Exception Flow 1:  
If the song is unavailable (e.g., removed, restricted, or not yet released), the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**UC-5: Listen to Playlist**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener initiates playback of a playlist. The system plays the songs in sequence (or shuffle mode, if enabled) and allows full playback control during listening.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet, or the playlist has been downloaded.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the playlist database.  
PRE-3: At least one song in the playlist is available for playback.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is being played on the user’s device, beginning with the first song or a selected song.  
POST-2: Play events for individual songs are recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can view the playlist or song details (UC-2 or UC-1), add a song to favourites (UC-6).    
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove song to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29). They can also download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener selects a playlist from a playlist details view, profile view, home view, recommended view, trending view, or search view.  
2\. The system begins playback of the playlist.  
3\. The listener interacts with the playback interface, which includes options to: Pause/resume playback, Skip to next/previous song, Seek within the current track, Enable/disable repeat (track or entire playlist), Adjust volume, View upcoming tracks (playlist queue), Toggle shuffle.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist is unavailable (deleted, private, or contains no playable songs), the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**UC-6: Add Song to Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener adds a song to their list of favourites. This action updates the user’s personal library and increases the favourite count on the song’s metadata.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet, or the song is already downloaded.  
PRE-2: The song exists in the music database.  
PRE-3: The listener is signed in to their account.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The song is added to the listener’s favourites list.  
POST-2: The favourite count for the song is incremented in the system.  
POST-3: The user may later remove the song from favourites (UC-7).  
POST-4: The favourite is reflected in relevant views such as the listener's profile (UC-3), favourites playlist, or home view.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener taps the 'favourite' icon on a song from: song detail view (UC-1), playlist view (UC-2), search results, or currently playing interface.  
2\. The system updates the song as a favourite for the user.  
3\. The favourite icon reflects the updated status, and confirmation feedback is shown (e.g., toast message or animation).

Exception Flow 1:  
If the song is no longer available or the listener has lost connection, an error message is displayed. The favourite action is not completed, and the user may retry when conditions are met.

**UC-7: Remove Song from Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener removes a previously favourited song from their list. This updates the user’s library and decreases the song’s favourite count system-wide.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The song exists in the music database.  
PRE-3: The song is already favourited by the listener.  
PRE-4: The listener is signed in to their account.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The song is removed from the listener’s favourites list.  
POST-2: The favourite count for the song is decremented in the system.  
POST-3: The user may re-add the song to favourites at any time (UC-6).  
POST-4: The change is reflected in any view where the song is shown, including profile (UC-3) or playlist views (UC-2).

Normal Flow:

The listener taps the active 'favourite' icon on a song from: song detail view (UC-1), playlist view (UC-2), search results, or currently playing interface.

The system removes the song from the listener's favourites.

The favourite icon reflects the updated status, and confirmation feedback is shown (e.g., toast message or animation).

Exception Flow 1:  
If the song is unavailable or there is a network issue, the system shows an appropriate error. The unfavourite action is not processed, and the user can retry later.

**UC-8: Add Playlist to Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener adds a public playlist to their list of favourites for quicker future access.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the playlist database and is public. Alternatively, the listener is a Premium Listener and the playlist is their own.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The selected playlist appears in the listener’s favourites list.  
POST-2: A "favourite playlist" event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can view the playlist (UC-2), play it (UC-5), or remove it from favourites later (UC-9).

Normal Flow:

The listener navigates to a playlist details page from search, profile, home view, or external link (UC-2).

The listener taps the "Add to Favourites" button/icon.

The system adds the playlist to the listener's favourites and updates the UI to reflect the change.

Alternative Flow 1:

The listener adds a playlist to favourites directly from a playlist preview card (e.g., in search results or home view).

The playlist is added without needing to view the detailed page.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist has been deleted, made private, or is unavailable, the system shows an error message and the operation is not completed.

**UC-9: Remove Playlist from Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener removes a previously favourited playlist from their list of favourites.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The playlist is currently in the listener’s favourites list.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is removed from the listener’s favourites.  
POST-2: A "remove favourite playlist" event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can still view or play the playlist if it remains public and available (UC-2, UC-5).

Normal Flow:

The listener accesses a playlist detail page from their favourites list or profile view.

The listener taps the "Remove from Favourites" button/icon.

The system removes the playlist from the listener's favourites and updates the UI accordingly.

Alternative Flow 1:

The listener removes the playlist directly from a favourites overview list or playlist preview.

The playlist is removed without needing to open the details page.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist no longer exists or the listener’s session has expired, the system notifies the user and the operation fails gracefully.

**UC-10: Add Profile to Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener chooses to add another listener’s public profile to their list of favourite profiles, for quick future access.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The target profile exists in the system and is public.  
PRE-3: The target profile is not already in the listener’s favourites list.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The profile is added to the listener’s favourites.  
POST-2: A favourite action is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The UI updates to reflect the profile is now favourited.  
POST-4: The listener can unfavourite the profile (UC-11), return to previous screen, or return to home screen.

Normal Flow:

The listener views the target profile via a profile detail view (UC-3).

The listener selects the “favourite” or “follow” button.

The system confirms the action and updates the interface.

Exception Flow 1:

If the profile is no longer available or has become private, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or home screen.

**UC-11: Remove Profile from Favourites**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener removes a previously favourited profile from their list of favourites.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The target profile exists and is in the listener’s favourites list.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The profile is removed from the listener’s favourites.  
POST-2: An unfavourite action is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The UI updates to reflect the profile is no longer favourited.  
POST-4: The listener can re-favourite the profile (UC-10), return to the previous screen, or return to home screen.

Normal Flow:

The listener views the target profile via profile detail view (UC-3) or through their favourites list.

The listener selects the “unfavourite” or “unfollow” button.

The system confirms the action and updates the interface.

Exception Flow 1:

If the profile is already unfavourited or unavailable, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can return to the previous screen or home screen.

**UC-12: Search Songs**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener initiates a search query and selects "Songs" to view a list of matching songs. The system displays search results with basic song metadata (title, artist, duration, album) and allows access to additional actions.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The song database is available and operational.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays a list of matching songs with basic metadata.  
POST-2: A search event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can tap a song to view details (UC-1), listen to the song (UC-4), or favourite it (UC-6).  
POST-4: Premium listeners can also add/remove the song to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) or download it (UC-24).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the search interface from the home screen.  
2\. The listener enters a search term.  
3\. The system defaults to showing results under “Songs”  
4\. The system presents matching songs, sorted by relevance.  
5\. The listener may select a song to perform one of the supported actions.

Exception Flow 1:

If no results are found, the system displays a “No songs found” message and suggests alternative search tips.

**UC-13: Search Playlists**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener performs a search and selects the “Playlists” category to browse matching playlists. The system returns playlists with basic details (title, owner, song count) and allows interaction with them.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The playlist database is available and operational.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays a list of matching playlists with basic metadata.  
POST-2: A search event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can tap a playlist to view details (UC-2), listen to the playlist (UC-5), or favourite it (UC-8).  
POST-4: Premium listeners can update/delete their own playlists (UC-29/UC-30) or change visibility settings (UC-31).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the search interface and enters a search term.  
2\. The listener switches to the “Playlists” tab.  
3\. The system presents playlists matching the search, sorted by relevance.  
4\. The listener may select a playlist to perform one of the supported actions.

Exception Flow 1:

If no matching playlists are found, the system shows a “No playlists found” message and suggests exploring trending playlists (UC-16).

**UC-14: Search Profiles**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener searches for user profiles by name or other identifier. The system displays matching user profiles with basic information (profile name, picture) and provides access to profile interactions.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The profile database is available and operational.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays matching user profiles with basic metadata.  
POST-2: A search event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can tap a profile to view its details (UC-3), follow/unfollow it (UC-10/UC-11), or explore its public playlists (UC-2).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the search interface and enters a name or keyword.  
2\. The listener switches to the “Profiles” tab.  
3\. The system displays profiles matching the query, sorted by relevance.  
4\. The listener selects a profile to view or interact with.

Exception Flow 1:  
If no matching profiles are found, the system shows a “No profiles found” message and suggests popular or trending profiles (UC-17/UC-20).

##### 

**UC-15: View Trending Songs**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener views a curated list of trending songs. This list reflects what is currently popular based on aggregate listener data and appears on the home view. Songs are displayed with basic metadata and allow further interaction.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: Trending data is available from the analytics system.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays a ranked or sorted list of trending songs.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can select any song to: view its details (UC-1), listen to it (UC-4), add/remove it to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), or return to the homescreen.  
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove a trending song to/from their playlists (UC-28/UC-29) and download it (UC-24/UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener opens the home view, trending section.  
2\. The listener can scroll through and interact with the songs.

Exception Flow 1:  
If trending data cannot be loaded due to connectivity or service error, the system shows an appropriate error message. The user can retry, return to the homescreen, or navigate elsewhere.

**UC-16: View Trending Playlists**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener views a curated list of trending playlists, compiled based on activity and popularity among all users. This content is displayed in the home view or can be accessed via a dedicated trending section.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: Trending playlist data is available from the analytics system.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system displays a list of trending playlists with associated metadata.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can choose to: view playlist details (UC-2), play the playlist (UC-5), favourite the playlist (UC-8), or go to the homescreen.  
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove songs from the playlist (if owner, UC-29), and download it (UC-26/UC-27).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the home view at the trending section.  
2\. A list of trending playlists is displayed with brief metadata.  
3\. The listener interacts with any playlist from the list.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the trending playlists cannot be retrieved, an error is shown. The listener can retry or return to the homescreen.

**UC-17: View Trending profiles**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener explores a list of trending profiles based on listener engagement metrics such as recent plays, follows, and shares. The section is accessible from the home view or a dedicated trending tab.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: Trending profile data is available from the analytics system.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The system presents a list of trending profiles.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can select an profile to: view profile details (UC-3), follow/unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), explore associated songs or playlists (UC-1/UC-2), or return to the homescreen.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the home screen at the trending section.  
2\. A list of trending profiles is displayed.  
3\. The listener scrolls through and selects an profile to view more details.

Exception Flow 1:  
If trending profile data fails to load, the system displays an error message. The listener may retry, go back, or return to the homescreen.

**UC-18: View Recommended Songs**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener accesses the home screen to view a list of recommended songs. These recommendations are personalized using listener data (e.g., listening history, favourites, similar users' behaviour) and are updated regularly.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The listener is signed in.  
PRE-3: The recommendation system has generated results based on available user data.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The recommended songs are displayed in a scrollable list or carousel format on the home screen.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can interact with each recommended song by: viewing its details (UC-1), playing the song (UC-4), adding/removing it from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), or accessing the artist's profile (UC-3).  
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove the song to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29), and download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

Normal Flow:

The listener navigates to the home screen.

The system loads the "Recommended for You" section with personalized song suggestions.

The listener browses through the list and selects a song to interact with as desired.

Exception Flow 1:  
If no recommendations are available (e.g., new user, insufficient data, or system error), the system displays a fallback message (e.g., “Start listening to get recommendations”) or defaults to trending content (UC-15). The user can still return to the homescreen or previous screen.

**UC-19: View Recommended Playlists**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener views a curated list of playlists recommended based on their listening habits, favourite songs, similar user preferences, and engagement history. This list is displayed on the home screen under a “Recommended Playlists” section.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The listener is signed in.  
PRE-3: The recommendation engine has returned playlist suggestions based on user data.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A list of recommended playlists is displayed on the home screen.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can interact with any playlist by: viewing playlist details (UC-2), playing the playlist (UC-5), favoriting the playlist (UC-8), or accessing the playlist owner's profile (UC-3).  
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add/remove songs from these playlists to their own playlists (UC-28/UC-29), and download the playlist (UC-26).

Normal Flow:

The listener opens the home screen.

The “Recommended Playlists” section is populated and shown to the listener.

The listener browses and selects a playlist to interact with.

Exception Flow 1:  
If no recommendations are available, the section is either hidden or replaced with a message (e.g., “Start exploring music to get playlist recommendations”). The listener may return to the homescreen or explore other sections.

**UC-20: View Recommended profiles**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
The system displays a set of profile profiles on the home screen that are recommended based on the listener’s behaviour (e.g., frequent genres, favourite songs, profiles followed by similar users). These help listeners discover new or related profiles.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The listener is signed in.  
PRE-3: The recommendation engine has generated profile suggestions using listener data.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The recommended profiles are shown on the home screen.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can interact with a profile by viewing their profile (UC-3), following/unfollowing (UC-10/UC-11), or playing their popular songs or playlists (UC-4/UC-5).  
POST-4: Premium Listeners can also add songs to playlists (UC-28) and download songs (UC-24).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the home screen  
2\. A “Recommended profiles” section displays a curated list of suggested profiles.  
3\. The listener selects a profile to interact with, such as visiting their profile or starting playback.

Exception Flow 1:  
If there are no personalized recommendations, the section may be hidden or replaced with trending profiles (UC-17). The listener can continue exploring the home screen.

**UC-21: Share Song**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener shares a song with others via a system-generated link or directly through supported third-party platforms such as messaging apps, social media, or email.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The song exists in the music database and is available.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A shareable link to the song is generated by the system.  
POST-2: The listener can copy the link or directly share it through available third-party options.  
POST-3: A share event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener selects the share option from the song details view or playback view.  
2\. The system generates a unique shareable link for the song.  
3\. The listener chooses to either copy the link or share it using an external app (e.g., messaging app, social media).

Exception Flow 1:  
If the song is unavailable or has been removed, the system displays an error message. The listener may return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**UC-22: Share Playlist**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener shares a playlist by generating a shareable link or using built-in sharing tools for supported external platforms.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the database and is public. Alternatively, the listener is the owner of the playlist.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A unique shareable link to the playlist is generated.  
POST-2: The listener can copy or share the link through third-party platforms.  
POST-3: A share event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:

1\. The listener selects the share option from the playlist details view.  
2\. The system generates a unique shareable link for the playlist.  
3\. The listener copies the link or shares it using an external platform.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist is private, deleted, or unavailable, the system displays an appropriate error message. The listener can return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**UC-23: Share Profile**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener shares a user profile by generating a shareable link or using system-supported third-party sharing mechanisms.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The profile exists and is public. Alternatively, the profile is the listener’s own.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A shareable profile link is generated by the system.  
POST-2: The listener can copy or distribute the link via third-party services.  
POST-3: A share event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener selects the share option from a profile view.  
2\. The system creates a unique link to the profile.  
3\. The listener shares the link using a preferred method (copy or external app).

Exception Flow 1:  
If the profile is private, deleted, or inaccessible, the system shows an error message. The listener can return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**UC-24: Add Song to Downloads**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener chooses to download a song for offline playback. The system downloads the audio file and makes it accessible from the user's local library. Downloads persist across app restarts unless explicitly removed.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The listener is authenticated as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-3: The song exists in the music database and is available for download.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The song is downloaded and stored locally on the listener’s device.  
POST-2: A download event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The listener can access the downloaded song offline from their library.  
POST-4: The listener can later remove the song from downloads (UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener selects the download button for a song from the song details view, playlist view, or now-playing view.  
2\. The system begins downloading the song.  
3\. Upon completion, the song becomes available in the downloads section of the listener’s library.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the download fails (e.g., due to insufficient storage, network failure, or content restrictions), the system displays an appropriate error message. The user can retry or return to the previous screen.

**UC-25: Remove Song from Downloads**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener chooses to remove a previously downloaded song from their local storage. This action frees device storage and removes offline playback access, though the song remains accessible online.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is authenticated as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-2: The song has been previously downloaded and exists in the local storage.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The song is removed from local device storage.  
POST-2: A removal event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The song is no longer available for offline playback.  
POST-4: The listener can later re-download the song (UC-24).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener navigates to their downloads section or the song detail view.  
2\. The listener selects the remove download option for the song.  
3\. The system removes the local copy of the song and updates the downloads list.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the song file cannot be removed (e.g., file not found or access issue), the system shows an appropriate error message and suggests corrective actions.

**UC-26: Add Playlist to Downloads**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener selects a playlist to download for offline access. The system downloads the playlist metadata and adds it to the listener’s Playlist Downloads list. All songs within the playlist are individually downloaded and added to the Song Downloads library.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is connected to the internet.  
PRE-2: The listener is authenticated as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-3: The playlist exists in the database and is public, or the listener is the owner.  
PRE-4: At least one song in the playlist is available for download.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is added to the listener’s Playlist Downloads.  
POST-2: All songs in the playlist are downloaded and added to the listener’s Song Downloads.  
POST-3: A playlist download event and corresponding song download events are recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-4: The listener can later remove the playlist from Playlist Downloads (UC-27). Songs remain available in Song Downloads unless removed individually (UC-25).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener selects the download option from a playlist detail view.  
2\. The system downloads playlist metadata and adds it to Playlist Downloads.  
3\. The system initiates download of all songs in the playlist, adding each to the listener’s Song Downloads.

Upon completion, the playlist and songs are accessible offline.

Exception Flow 1:  
If any songs cannot be downloaded (due to content restrictions, network failure, or storage issues), the system displays a partial download message. Successfully downloaded items remain available offline.

**UC-27: Remove Playlist from Downloads**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener chooses to remove a previously downloaded playlist from their Playlist Downloads. This action removes only the playlist reference; songs downloaded as part of it remain available in Song Downloads unless explicitly removed.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is authenticated as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the listener’s Playlist Downloads.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is removed from the listener’s Playlist Downloads.  
POST-2: A playlist removal event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: All previously downloaded songs remain in Song Downloads.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener navigates to the Playlist Downloads section or playlist detail view.  
2\. The listener selects the remove download option for the playlist.  
3\. The system removes the playlist reference from Playlist Downloads but retains all previously downloaded songs in Song Downloads.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist cannot be removed due to system error (e.g., sync failure or file access issues), the system displays an error message and suggests retry or alternative options. 

**UC-28: Create Playlist**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener creates a new playlist, providing a title and optionally a description and access level (Public, Private). The playlist is saved under the listener’s profile and can be edited later.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in and identified as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-2: The listener is connected to the internet.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A new playlist is created and saved in the system, owned by the listener.  
POST-2: The playlist appears in the listener’s profile and playlist library.  
POST-3: The listener may proceed to add songs (UC-29), edit playlist details (UC-29), or set its visibility level (UC-31).

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener navigates to their profile or library view.  
2\. The listener selects the “Create Playlist” option.  
3\. The system prompts for a playlist title, optional description, and optional access level.  
4\. The listener submits the form.  
5\. The system confirms creation and displays the empty playlist view.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the title is blank or invalid, the system prompts the listener to enter a valid title.

Exception Flow 2:  
If the playlist creation fails due to a server or connectivity issue, an error message is shown and the listener can retry.

**UC-29: Update Playlist**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener edits an existing playlist they own. This may include changing the title or description, reordering or removing songs, and adding songs from the catalog.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in and is the owner of the playlist.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists and is accessible.  
PRE-3: The listener is connected to the internet.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is updated with the listener’s changes.  
POST-2: The updated playlist appears immediately in the library and profile views.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses one of their playlists via profile or library.  
2\. The listener selects the “Edit Playlist” option.  
3\. The listener makes changes such as: Updating the title or description, Adding or removing songs, Reordering songs  
4\. The listener saves the changes.  
5\. The system updates the playlist and confirms the update.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist no longer exists or the listener is no longer the owner, an appropriate error message is shown.

Exception Flow 2:  
If the update fails due to a system error, the listener is informed and given the option to retry.

**UC-30: Delete Playlist**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener deletes a playlist they own. The playlist is removed from the listener’s profile and becomes inaccessible to others.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in and is the owner of the playlist.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists in the system.  
PRE-3: The listener is connected to the internet.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist is permanently deleted from the system.  
POST-2: Any references to the playlist are removed from the listener’s profile and analytics.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses one of their playlists via profile or library.  
2\. The listener selects the “Delete Playlist” option.  
3\. The system prompts for confirmation.  
4\. The listener confirms the deletion.  
5\. The playlist is deleted, and the listener is redirected to their library.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist does not exist or has already been deleted, the system shows an appropriate message.

Exception Flow 2:  
If the deletion fails due to a server issue, the listener is informed and can retry later.

**UC-31: Set Playlist Access Level**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener sets the visibility of a playlist they own. Available access levels are: Public (visible to all users), Private (visible only to the owner).

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in and is the owner of the playlist.  
PRE-2: The playlist exists and is accessible.  
PRE-3: The listener is connected to the internet.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The playlist’s access level is updated in the system.  
POST-2: The visibility of the playlist is adjusted according to the selected level.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses one of their playlists via profile or library.  
2\. The listener selects the “Edit Access Level” option.  
3\. The listener chooses one of the available visibility settings.  
4\. The system confirms the update.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the playlist does not exist or the listener is not the owner, an error is shown.

Exception Flow 2:  
If the access level change fails due to a system error, the listener is notified and can try again.

**UC-32: Sign Up**  
Actors: Guest

Description:  
A Guest user registers for an account to access listening features. The system collects required information and creates an account, allowing the user to become either a Basic Listener or a Premium Listener depending on their selection.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The Guest has access to the internet.  
PRE-2: The Guest is not currently signed in.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A new listener account is created and stored in the system.  
POST-2: The user is signed in and redirected to the onboarding or home screen.  
POST-3: A sign-up event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Guest selects the “Sign Up” option from the welcome or login screen.  
2\. The system presents a registration form requesting required information (e.g., email, password, display name, etc.).  
3\. The Guest fills out and submits the form.  
4\. The system validates the data and creates the user account.  
5\. The user is automatically signed in and redirected to onboarding or home view.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The Guest opts to sign up via third-party authentication (e.g., Google, Apple).  
2\. The system verifies credentials with the provider.  
3\. If successful, the account is created and the user is signed in.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the submitted data is invalid (e.g., email already in use, weak password), the system highlights the error and prompts for corrections.

**UC-33: Sign In**  
Actors: Guest

Description:  
A Guest user signs in to an existing account using registered credentials or a third-party authentication method.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The Guest has access to the internet.  
PRE-2: The user account exists in the system.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The user is authenticated and signed in.  
POST-2: A sign-in event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The user is redirected to the home view or last session state.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Guest selects “Sign In” from the welcome screen.  
2\. The system presents a sign-in form (email/username and password).  
3\. The Guest submits credentials.  
4\. The system verifies the credentials and signs the user in.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The Guest opts for third-party sign-in.  
2\. The system redirects and verifies with the third-party provider.  
3\. Upon success, the user is signed in.

Exception Flow 1:  
If credentials are invalid or authentication fails, the system displays an error message and prompts the user to retry.

**UC-34: Sign Out**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A signed-in user signs out of the application, terminating the current session on the device.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The user is signed in.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The session is ended and user data is cleared from the device.  
POST-2: The user is redirected to the welcome or login screen.  
POST-3: A sign-out event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The user selects the “Sign Out” option from the settings or account menu.  
2\. The system confirms the action and ends the session.  
3\. The user is returned to the welcome screen.

Exception Flow 1:  
If there is a network or server issue during sign-out, the system still clears the local session and notifies the user of possible issues syncing with the server.

**UC-35: Recover Account**  
Actors: Guest

Description:  
A Guest attempts to recover access to an existing account by requesting a password reset or reactivation link.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The Guest has access to the internet.  
PRE-2: The user account exists and supports recovery via email or linked third-party service.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A recovery link or code is sent to the user’s email or third-party account.  
POST-2: The user can reset their password or regain account access.  
POST-3: A recovery attempt is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Guest selects “Forgot Password” from the sign-in screen.  
2\. The system prompts for the account’s registered email or username.  
3\. The Guest submits the information.  
4\. The system sends a secure recovery link or code.  
5\. The Guest uses the link to reset the password and sign in.

Alternative Flow 1:  
1\. The user chooses to recover their account using a linked third-party login.  
2.The system redirects to the provider and, upon verification, allows reaccess.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the submitted email or username is not found, the system informs the user and allows another attempt.

**UC-36: Delete Account**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A signed-in listener permanently deletes their account, removing all associated data, including playlists, favourites, profile information, and listening history.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in.  
PRE-2: The listener confirms the account deletion action through a secure confirmation flow.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The user account and associated data are permanently removed from the system.  
POST-2: The listener is signed out and redirected to the welcome screen.  
POST-3: A deletion event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener navigates to account settings.  
2\. The listener selects the “Delete Account” option.  
3\. The system displays a warning and requests confirmation (e.g., password re-entry or verification code).  
4\. The listener confirms the deletion.  
5\. The system deletes the account and all associated data.

The user is signed out and returned to the welcome screen.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the listener cancels the deletion or fails to confirm identity, the deletion process is aborted and no data is removed.

Exception Flow 2:  
If there is a server error or failure during deletion, the system displays an error message and instructs the listener to retry later.

**UC-37: Adjust Account Settings**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener modifies personal account settings such as display name, profile picture, email address, password, and notification preferences.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The updated account settings are saved to the system.  
POST-2: A settings update event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener navigates to account settings via the settings or profile menu.  
2\. The listener selects a field to edit (e.g., display name, email, password, notification preferences).  
3\. The system presents the relevant input form.  
4\. The listener submits changes.  
5\. The system validates and applies the updates.  
6\. A confirmation is displayed to the user.

Alternative Flow 1:

1\. If the listener updates a sensitive field (e.g., password or email), the system may require re-authentication before applying changes.  
2\. They can proceed as usual.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the submitted information is invalid (e.g., email already in use, weak password), the system displays appropriate error messages and prompts for correction.

**UC-38: View Subscription Details**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener views their current subscription status, including type (Basic or Premium), renewal date, billing details, and benefits overview.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: Subscription details are displayed on the user’s device.  
POST-2: A view event is recorded in the analytics system.

Normal Flow:

The listener navigates to the “Subscription” section via account or settings menu.

The system retrieves and displays subscription details, including plan type, status (active/inactive), renewal or expiration date, and benefits summary.

Exception Flow 1:  
If subscription data is unavailable due to a system error, the system displays an appropriate message and provides contact support options.

**UC-39: Activate Subscription**  
Actors: Basic Listener

Description:  
A Basic Listener initiates a Premium subscription. The system processes the payment, updates the user’s account, and grants Premium access.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The user is signed in as a Basic Listener.  
PRE-2: A valid payment method is provided.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The listener’s account is upgraded to Premium status.  
POST-2: A subscription activation event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The user gains immediate access to Premium-only features.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Basic Listener selects “Upgrade to Premium” from the subscription section.  
2\. The system displays available Premium plans and associated benefits.  
3\. The listener selects a plan and provides payment details.  
4\. The system processes payment and updates the account to Premium.  
5\. The system confirms activation and shows Premium status.

Exception Flow 1:  
If payment fails, the system informs the listener and prompts for an alternative method.

Exception Flow 2:  
If the user already has an active Premium subscription, the system prevents duplicate activation and informs the user.

**UC-40: Cancel Subscription**  
Actors: Premium Listener

Description:  
A Premium Listener cancels their recurring subscription. The system updates the subscription status and schedules reversion to Basic at the end of the billing period.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The user is signed in as a Premium Listener.  
PRE-2: The user has an active Premium subscription.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: The subscription is marked for cancellation at the end of the billing cycle.  
POST-2: A cancellation event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The user retains Premium access until the expiration date.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The Premium Listener goes to the subscription section.  
2\. The listener selects the option to cancel the subscription.  
3\. The system displays a confirmation prompt explaining the impact of cancellation.  
4\. The listener confirms.  
5\. The system marks the subscription to end at the billing cycle’s conclusion and notifies the user.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the cancellation fails due to system error, the system displays an error message and provides retry or support options.

**UC-41: Renew Subscription**  
Actors: Basic Listener, Premium Listener

Description:  
A listener with a previously cancelled or expired Premium subscription renews it by selecting a new plan and completing payment.

Preconditions:  
PRE-1: The listener is signed in.  
PRE-2: The listener does not have an active Premium subscription.

Postconditions:  
POST-1: A new Premium subscription is activated.  
POST-2: A renewal event is recorded in the analytics system.  
POST-3: The user gains immediate access to Premium features.

Normal Flow:  
1\. The listener accesses the subscription section.  
2\. The system detects no active Premium subscription and displays available plans.  
3\. The listener selects a plan and completes payment.  
4\. The system activates the subscription and updates the listener’s status.

Exception Flow 1:  
If the payment is unsuccessful, the system shows an error and prompts for an alternative method.

#### 

#### 

####   

*This page intentionally left blank*

# **2\. System Requirements Specification** {#2.-system-requirements-specification}

# ---

### 

## **2.1. Non-functional requirements** {#2.1.-non-functional-requirements}

The Non-functional requirements define the system behavior, based on each use case. To fulfill our goals, we decided to prioritize documenting the 5 NFRs for each Use case: Performance, Availability, Usability, Security and Scalability as the most important NFRs for user experience. Details of each NFRs:

**NFRs for UC-1: View Song Detail**  
Performance: The song details page must load within 1.5 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The song metadata viewing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Metadata must be presented in a clear, readable layout, with section headings (e.g., Artist, Album, Lyrics) and responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must be able to support at least 10,000 concurrent requests to view song details without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-2: View Playlist Details**  
Performance: The playlist details page must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The playlist metadata viewing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Playlist metadata must be presented in a structured, scrollable layout with distinct sections (e.g., Title, Description, Song List) and a responsive design supporting both mobile and desktop devices.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token. Unauthorized access attempts to private or deleted playlists must trigger an appropriate error message.

Scalability: The system must support at least 10,000 concurrent requests to view playlist details without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-3: View Profile Details**  
Performance: The profile details page must load within 1.5 seconds on a stable 4G connection.  
Availability: The profile viewing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Profile details must be presented in a clean, accessible layout, with profile name, picture, bio, and associated content clearly distinguished. The design must adapt to both mobile and desktop screens.

Security: All profile view requests must include a valid authentication token. Attempts to access private or deleted profiles must result in an appropriate error message, without leaking any personal data.

Scalability: The system must be able to handle at least 10,000 concurrent requests to view profiles without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-4: Listen to Song**  
Performance: The song must begin playback within 1 second after the listener initiates play on a stable 4G connection. Playback buffering should not exceed 2 seconds under normal conditions.

Availability: The song playback feature must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure uninterrupted streaming.

Usability: Playback controls (play, pause, seek, shuffle, repeat, volume) must be easily accessible and responsive across mobile and desktop devices, with real-time updates to playback progress.

Security: All playback requests must include a valid authentication token. DRM (Digital Rights Management) and regional restrictions must be enforced at the time of playback initiation.

Scalability: The system must support up to 50,000 concurrent song playback sessions without degradation in streaming quality.

**NFRs for UC-5: Listen to Playlist**  
Performance: The playlist must begin playback within 2 seconds after initiation on a stable 4G connection. Transition between tracks must occur seamlessly, with a gap of no more than 500 milliseconds.

Availability: The playlist playback feature must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure reliable listening experiences.

Usability: The playlist playback interface must provide clear controls for playlist navigation (next, previous, shuffle, repeat), and must visually indicate the current track and upcoming queue. The interface must be optimized for both mobile and desktop platforms.

Security: All playlist playback requests must include a valid authentication token. Access to playlists and songs must comply with content licensing and regional restrictions.

Scalability: The system must support at least 30,000 concurrent playlist playback sessions without degradation in performance or streaming quality.

**NFRs for UC-6: Add Song to Favourites**  
Performance: The system must update the song's favourite status and reflect it in the UI within 1 second of user action under a stable internet connection.

Availability: The favourite feature must maintain 99.9% uptime to ensure consistent user access.

Usability: The favourite action must provide immediate visual confirmation (e.g., icon change, toast message) and be accessible from all supported views with a consistent interface.

Security: The action must only be permitted if the listener is authenticated with a valid token. Unauthorized requests must be rejected with appropriate messages.

Scalability: The system must handle at least 20,000 concurrent favourite actions without degradation in response times.

**NFRs for UC-7: Remove Song from Favourites**  
Performance: The system must process the removal of the song from favourites and reflect the change in the UI within 1 second of user action on a stable connection.

Availability: The unfavourite feature must maintain 99.9% uptime to support user interactions reliably.

Usability: The removal action must provide clear feedback (e.g., icon update, toast notification) and be consistent across all supported views.

Security: Only authenticated listeners may remove songs from their favourites. Invalid or expired sessions must block the action and prompt re-authentication.

Scalability: The system must support at least 20,000 concurrent unfavourite actions without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-8: Add Playlist to Favourites**  
Performance: The system must update the playlist's favourite status and reflect it in the UI within 1 second of user action under a stable internet connection.

Availability: The playlist favourite feature must maintain 99.9% uptime to ensure uninterrupted access.

Usability: The action must offer immediate confirmation feedback (e.g., icon change, animation), and the option must be consistently available across playlist detail and preview views.

Security: Only authenticated users can add playlists to their favourites. The system must enforce access checks based on playlist visibility and listener privileges.

Scalability: The system must support at least 15,000 concurrent playlist favourite actions without impacting system performance.

**NFRs for UC-9: Remove Playlist from Favourites**  
Performance: The system must process the removal of the playlist from favourites and reflect the change in the UI within 1 second of user action under normal network conditions.

Availability: The unfavourite playlist feature must maintain 99.9% uptime for reliability.

Usability: The system must provide visual confirmation (e.g., updated icon, message) of the playlist being removed from favourites, consistent across all supported views.

Security: The action must only be available to authenticated users. Invalid or expired sessions must block the action and trigger a login requirement.

Scalability: The system must handle up to 15,000 concurrent unfavourite playlist actions without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-10: Add Profile to Favourites**  
Performance: The system must process and reflect the addition of a profile to favourites within 1 second of user action under a stable connection.

Availability: The favourite profile feature must maintain 99.9% uptime for seamless user experiences.

Usability: The favourite action must be clearly presented (e.g., button or icon) and must provide immediate feedback upon completion (e.g., icon change, message).

Security: Only authenticated users can favourite profiles. The system must verify the target profile’s status and visibility before allowing the action.

Scalability: The system must support at least 10,000 concurrent profile favourite actions without any degradation in user experience.

**NFRs for UC-11: Remove Profile from Favourites**  
Performance: The system must process the removal of a profile from favourites and update the UI within 1 second after user action under stable conditions.

Availability: The unfavourite profile feature must maintain 99.9% uptime to support reliable interactions.

Usability: The action must provide instant confirmation (e.g., icon update, toast notification), consistent across profile detail and favourites views.

Security: The action must be restricted to authenticated listeners. Expired sessions must prevent the action and prompt for login.

Scalability: The system must support at least 10,000 concurrent profile unfavourite actions without degrading the system’s responsiveness.

**NFRs for UC-12: Search Songs**  
Performance:The system must return search results within 2 seconds for typical queries under a stable internet connection. The search engine must support typo tolerance and partial matching with minimal latency.

Availability: The search functionality must maintain 99.9% uptime. The song metadata database must be highly available to ensure accurate search results.

Usability:  
The search interface must be intuitive, with category tabs clearly visible (defaulting to “Songs”). Search results must display key metadata (title, artist, album, duration) in a clean, scrollable format.

Scalability:  
The system must handle at least 50,000 concurrent song search queries without impacting performance.

Security:  
Only authorized and authenticated listeners can access full song actions (e.g., play, favourite, download). The system must protect user search history as private data and comply with data privacy policies.

**NFRs for UC-13: Search Playlists**  
Performance: The system must display playlist search results within 2 seconds under normal conditions. Sorting by relevance must factor in playlist popularity, recency, and ownership.

Availability: The playlist search functionality must maintain 99.9% uptime. The playlist metadata database must be accessible and up to date at all times.

Usability: The search UI must allow easy switching between “Songs” and “Playlists” tabs. Results must present playlist metadata (title, owner, song count) clearly, with smooth scrolling.

Scalability: The system must handle up to 30,000 concurrent playlist search requests.

Security: Listeners can only modify, delete, or adjust visibility of their own playlists. The system must enforce visibility settings, ensuring private playlists are never exposed in search results to unauthorized users.

**NFRs for UC-14: Search Profiles**  
Performance: The system must return profile search results within 2 seconds for standard queries. The system must support both exact and fuzzy name matching.

Availability: Profile search functionality must maintain 99.9% uptime. The profile database must be highly available and consistent.

Usability: The system must display profiles with essential metadata (profile name, picture) in a user-friendly, scrollable format. The system must provide helpful suggestions (e.g., trending profiles) if no matches are found.  
Scalability: The system must support at least 20,000 concurrent profile search requests.

Security: Only public profiles should appear in search results unless the user is the owner. Profile data must comply with privacy settings and data protection regulations.

**NFRs for UC-15: View Trending Songs**  
Performance: The trending songs section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The trending songs feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Trending songs must be presented in a clear, scrollable list with visible ranking or sorting, and must support responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token where applicable. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must be able to support at least 8,000 concurrent requests to view trending songs without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-16: View Trending Playlists**  
Performance: The trending playlists section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The trending playlists feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Trending playlists must be displayed with concise metadata in a clean, scrollable interface, ensuring responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token where applicable. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must support at least 7,500 concurrent requests to view trending playlists without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-17: View Trending Profiles**  
Performance: The trending profiles section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The trending profiles feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Profiles must be presented in an intuitive grid or list layout with avatars and key information visible, supporting responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token where applicable. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must support at least 7,500 concurrent requests to view trending profiles without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-18: View Recommended Songs**  
Performance: The recommended songs section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The recommendation feature for songs must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Recommended songs must be displayed in a visually appealing carousel or list format with actionable buttons, ensuring responsiveness across mobile and desktop devices.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must support at least 8,000 concurrent requests to view recommended songs without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-19: View Recommended Playlists**  
Performance: The recommended playlists section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The playlist recommendation feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Recommended playlists must be presented in a clear, scrollable format with essential metadata and cover art, ensuring responsive design for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must support at least 7,500 concurrent requests to view recommended playlists without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-20: View Recommended Profiles**  
Performance: The recommended profiles section must load within 2 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The profile recommendation feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: Recommended profiles must be displayed with avatars and key highlights in a scrollable and responsive layout for mobile and desktop.

Security: All requests must include a valid authentication token. Expired tokens trigger a session termination and redirect.

Scalability: The system must support at least 7,500 concurrent requests to view recommended profiles without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-21: Share Song**  
Performance: The shareable link must be generated and displayed within 1 second on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The song sharing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The share options (copy link, share via app) must be presented in a clear and user-friendly interface, ensuring intuitive flow and responsive design on both mobile and desktop devices.

Security: All requests to generate share links must include a valid authentication token. Links must contain obfuscated identifiers to prevent enumeration. Expired tokens must result in session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must support at least 5,000 concurrent share requests without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-22: Share Playlist**  
Performance: The playlist shareable link must be generated and displayed within 1 second on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The playlist sharing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The sharing interface must display clear options to copy the link or share via external platforms, ensuring ease of use and consistent behavior across devices.

Security: All sharing actions must include a valid authentication token. Private playlists must not expose shareable links unless the listener is the owner. Invalid or expired tokens trigger session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must be able to handle at least 5,000 concurrent requests for playlist sharing without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-23: Share Profile**  
Performance: The profile shareable link must be generated and displayed within 1 second on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The profile sharing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The user interface must present the share options (copy link, share via third-party platforms) clearly, ensuring consistent and user-friendly behavior on both desktop and mobile devices.

Security: All profile share actions must require a valid authentication token. Private profiles must not expose shareable links. Any invalid session or token must result in a session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must be capable of supporting at least 5,000 concurrent profile share actions without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-24: Add Song to Downloads**  
Performance: The download initiation must begin within 1 second of user action. The download speed must match at least 80% of the user’s available bandwidth on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The download service must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The download button must provide clear feedback (progress indicator, success/failure status) in a user-friendly and responsive design across mobile and desktop.

Security: All download requests must require the listener to be authenticated as a Premium Listener with a valid session token. Any unauthorized or expired token must result in session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must support at least 3,000 concurrent download initiations without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-25: Remove Song from Downloads**  
Performance: The removal of a downloaded song must be processed and reflected in the downloads list within 1 second.

Availability: The download management feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The remove download option must provide clear confirmation prompts and success/error feedback, ensuring intuitive operation on all supported devices.

Security: Only authenticated Premium Listeners can remove downloads. All removal requests must validate the active Premium session token. Expired or invalid tokens result in session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must handle at least 3,000 concurrent download removal actions without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-26: Add Playlist to Downloads**  
Performance: Playlist metadata must be added to downloads within 1.5 seconds. Song downloads must initiate immediately after playlist download is confirmed, with progress tracking per song.

Availability: The playlist download service must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The user interface must present playlist download progress clearly, with detailed status per song and graceful handling of partial downloads, ensuring consistent behavior on mobile and desktop.

Security: Only authenticated Premium Listeners can add playlists to downloads. All requests must validate Premium session tokens. Private playlists may only be downloaded by their owners. Invalid or expired tokens must result in session termination and redirection.
