Scalability: The system must support at least 3,000 concurrent playlist download initiations, including the simultaneous download of up to 100 songs per playlist, without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-27: Remove Playlist from Downloads**  
Performance: The removal of a playlist reference from downloads must be completed and reflected in the UI within 1 second.

Availability: The playlist download management feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The remove playlist from downloads option must provide confirmation prompts and clear feedback upon completion or error, ensuring usability across all supported platforms.

Security: Only authenticated Premium Listeners can remove downloaded playlists. All actions must validate the Premium session token. Invalid or expired tokens result in session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must handle at least 3,000 concurrent playlist removal actions without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-28: Create Playlist**  
Performance: The playlist creation process, including saving and displaying the new playlist view, must complete within 1 second after submission.

Availability: The playlist creation feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The create playlist interface must provide clear prompts for title, description, and access level, along with real-time validation and feedback on errors (e.g., blank or invalid title) across mobile and desktop.

Security: Only authenticated Premium Listeners can create playlists. All creation requests must validate the Premium session token. Unauthorized or expired tokens must trigger session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must support at least 5,000 concurrent playlist creation requests without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-29: Update Playlist**  
Performance: The playlist update must be processed and reflected in the user interface within 1 second after submission.

Availability: The playlist editing feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The playlist editing interface must provide intuitive controls for editing title, description, song management (add/remove/reorder), and confirm updates with clear success or error feedback on all supported devices.

Security: Only the playlist owner, authenticated as a Premium Listener, can update the playlist. All update requests must verify the Premium session token and ownership. Invalid or expired tokens must result in session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must handle at least 5,000 concurrent playlist update actions without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-30: Delete Playlist**  
Performance: The playlist deletion process must be completed and reflected in the library view within 1 second after confirmation.

Availability: The playlist deletion feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The delete playlist interface must include clear confirmation prompts and provide immediate feedback upon success or failure, ensuring consistency across devices.

Security: Only the authenticated Premium Listener who owns the playlist can delete it. All deletion requests must validate the Premium session token and ownership. Invalid or expired tokens must trigger session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must support at least 5,000 concurrent playlist deletion requests without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-31: Set Playlist Access Level**  
Performance: Changing the playlist access level must be processed and reflected within 1 second after user selection.

Availability: The playlist access level management feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The access level interface must present options (Public, Private) in a user-friendly format with clear feedback upon update or error, ensuring responsive behavior across devices.

Security: Only the authenticated Premium Listener who owns the playlist can change its access level. All change requests must validate the Premium session token and ownership. Invalid or expired tokens must trigger session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must handle at least 5,000 concurrent access level update requests without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-32: Sign Up**  
Performance: The registration process, including validation and account creation, must complete within 2 seconds after form submission on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The sign-up service must maintain 99.9% uptime to ensure continuous accessibility for new users.

Usability: The registration form must be clear, guiding the user with field labels, validation messages, and progress indicators, and be responsive for mobile and desktop devices.

Security: All registration requests must be transmitted over HTTPS, and password fields must enforce secure password policies (minimum length, complexity). Third-party authentication must follow the provider’s latest security guidelines.

Scalability: The system must be able to handle at least 5,000 concurrent sign-up attempts without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-33: Sign In**  
Performance: User authentication and session initialization must complete within 1.5 seconds after credential submission on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The sign-in feature must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure reliable access to the service.

Usability: Error messages for invalid credentials must be clear and non-technical. The sign-in form must support accessibility standards and responsive design.

Security: All authentication processes must be secured using HTTPS, with rate limiting in place to prevent brute-force attacks. Sessions must be securely managed with token expiration and renewal policies.

Scalability: The system must support at least 15,000 concurrent sign-in requests without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-34: Sign Out**  
Performance: The sign-out process, including session termination and redirection, must complete within 1 second on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The sign-out function must be available 99.95% of the time.

Usability: Confirmation and status messages must be clear and immediate, ensuring the user knows the session has ended successfully.

Security: Session termination must invalidate all authentication tokens, and residual session data on the device must be securely cleared.

Scalability: The system must be capable of handling at least 20,000 concurrent sign-out operations without impacting session integrity or system performance.

**NFRs fo UC-35: Recover Account**  
Performance: The account recovery email or verification link must be sent within 60 seconds of the user request on a stable connection.

Availability: The account recovery service must maintain 99.9% uptime to guarantee user support.

Usability:The recovery flow must provide clear instructions, status messages, and user-friendly error messages if data is invalid or not found.

Security: All recovery requests must be verified using secure tokens or codes that expire after 15 minutes. Attempts must be logged and rate-limited to prevent abuse.

Scalability: The system must support at least 3,000 concurrent recovery requests without affecting delivery performance or user experience.

**NFRs for UC-36: Delete Account**  
Performance: The account deletion process, including data purging and session termination, must complete within 3 seconds after final confirmation.

Availability: The account deletion service must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The deletion flow must include clear warnings, require confirmation through secure reauthentication, and display success or error messages appropriately.

Security: Deletion requests must require explicit confirmation, such as password re-entry or multi-factor authentication. Data deletion must comply with applicable data protection regulations.

Scalability: The system must be able to process at least 1,000 concurrent account deletions without impacting other services.

**NFRs for UC-37: Adjust Account Settings**  
Performance: The update of account settings must complete within 1.5 seconds upon submission on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The account settings feature must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure users can manage their information at any time.

Usability: The settings interface must be intuitive, segmented into logical sections (e.g., Profile Info, Security, Notifications), and support mobile and desktop access.

Security: Changes to sensitive fields (e.g., password, email) must trigger reauthentication and comply with password policies. All data transmissions must occur over HTTPS.

Scalability: The system must support at least 10,000 concurrent settings update operations without affecting other core services.

**NFRs for UC-38: View Subscription Details**  
Performance: The subscription details page must load within 1.5 seconds on a stable 4G connection for online content.

Availability: The subscription details feature must maintain 99.9% uptime.

Usability: The subscription details page must present information in a clear, organized layout with logical section headings (e.g., Subscription Type, Renewal Date, Billing Information, Benefits). The page must be responsive on both mobile and desktop devices.

Security: All requests to view subscription details must include a valid authentication token. Expired tokens will trigger session termination and redirection.

Scalability: The system must support at least 10,000 concurrent subscription details views without degradation in performance.

**NFRs for UC-39: Activate Subscription**  
Performance: The subscription activation process, including payment processing and account update, must complete within 3 seconds on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The subscription activation feature must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure availability for upgrading to Premium status.

Usability: The plan selection and payment submission process must be user-friendly, with clear instructions, progress indicators, and accessible error messages for invalid payment information or other issues.

Security: Payment processing must comply with PCI DSS standards, and sensitive payment information must be transmitted securely using HTTPS. All data must be securely stored and comply with applicable privacy regulations.

Scalability: The system must be able to handle at least 5,000 concurrent subscription activation requests without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-40: Cancel Subscription**  
Performance: The subscription cancellation process, including updating subscription status and notifying the user, must complete within 2 seconds.

Availability: The subscription cancellation service must maintain 99.95% uptime to ensure uninterrupted access to cancellation features.

Usability: The cancellation flow must clearly explain the impact of the cancellation (e.g., access duration) and include a confirmation prompt to avoid accidental cancellations. The user interface must be clear and easy to navigate.

Security: The cancellation process must ensure that only the account owner can perform the action. Requests to cancel must be validated with secure authentication.

Scalability: The system must support at least 3,000 concurrent subscription cancellation operations without performance degradation.

**NFRs for UC-41: Renew Subscription**  
Performance: The subscription renewal process, including payment processing and subscription update, must complete within 3 seconds on a stable 4G connection.

Availability: The subscription renewal service must maintain 99.95% uptime, ensuring that listeners can renew their subscription without disruption.

Usability: The renewal process must be intuitive and present clear options for plan selection. The payment flow should include simple error handling and guidance for payment issues.

Security: Payment information must be securely handled in compliance with PCI DSS standards. All communications involving payment must occur over HTTPS. The user must be re-authenticated before proceeding with payment.

Scalability: The system must support at least 5,000 concurrent subscription renewal operations without affecting performance.

## 

## **2.2. Functional Requirements** {#2.2.-functional-requirements}

The functional requirements outlined in this section are derived from detailed use case descriptions and are informed by relevant non-functional considerations. These requirements define the expected behavior of the system and serve as a foundation for system design, development, and testing activities. Each requirement is specified to ensure that the system meets user needs and supports all identified scenarios.

**FR for UC-1: View Song Details**  
FR1: Data Retrieval and Display  
FR1.1: When the listener selects the "info" option for a song from any supported context (e.g., search view, home view, playlist view, profile view), the client shall request song metadata from the server using the song's unique identifier.  
FR1.2: The server shall respond with a structured payload containing the song title, description, creation date, owner name, duration  
FR1.3: The client shall display the retrieved metadata in a detailed view layout and load the album art asynchronously to avoid blocking the UI.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: Upon successful display of song metadata, the client shall log a song detail view event by sending relevant data (including song identifier, user identifier, timestamp, and source context) to the analytics system.

FR3. Action Buttons (Non-Playback)  
FR3.1: The song detail screen shall provide user interface controls to:  
Navigate back to the previous screen, Return to the home screen, View the artist or owner profile (links to UC-3), View the associated playlist (if accessed from one; UC-2), Add or remove the song from favourites (UC-6, UC-7)

FR3.2: For listeners with Premium status, additional controls shall be provided to:  
Add or remove the song from their owned playlists (UC-28, UC-29)  
Download or remove the download of the song (UC-24, UC-25)

FR4. Song Playback  
FR4.1: The song detail page shall include a "Play" button that triggers a request to the playback module in accordance with UC-4.

FR5. Third-Party Access  
FR5.1: If the song detail page is accessed via an external link containing the song's identifier, the client shall parse the identifier and request the corresponding metadata as defined in FR1.

FR6. Error Handling  
FR6.1: If the server indicates that the song is unavailable or does not exist, the system shall display an error message: “Song unavailable,” along with options to return to the previous screen or go to the home screen.

**FR for UC-2: View Playlist Details**  
FR1. Playlist Metadata Retrieval and Display  
FR1.1: When the listener selects a playlist from any supported context such as home view, search view, or profile view, the client shall request playlist metadata from the server using the playlist’s unique identifier.  
FR1.2: The server shall respond with a structured payload containing the playlist title, description, total song count, creation date, owner name, and a list of all songs in the playlist, each including title, artist, duration, and album.  
FR1.3: The client shall render the playlist data in a detailed view layout.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: Upon successfully displaying the playlist details, the client shall send a playlist detail view event to the analytics system with metadata including playlist ID, user ID, timestamp, and source context.

FR3. Interactive Options  
FR3.1: The interface shall allow the listener to navigate back, return to the home screen, view the playlist owner’s profile (UC-3), view any individual song’s detail (UC-1), play the entire playlist (UC-5), add or remove individual songs from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), and add or remove the playlist from favourites (UC-8/UC-9).  
FR3.2: If the listener is a Premium Listener and the owner of the playlist, additional actions shall be available to update or delete the playlist (UC-29/UC-30) and to set its access level (UC-31).

FR4. Error Handling  
FR4.1: If the playlist is unavailable, private, or deleted, the system shall display an error message stating “Playlist unavailable,” and provide options to return to the previous screen or home screen.

**FR for UC-3: View Profile Details**  
FR1. Profile Metadata Retrieval and Display  
FR1.1: When the listener selects a profile, the client shall send a request to the server using the profile’s unique identifier.  
FR1.2: The server shall return a response containing the profile name, profile picture URL, optional bio, favourites count, and a list of public playlists.  
FR1.3: The client shall render this information in a formatted profile detail layout.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: The client shall log a profile view event upon successful display, including the profile ID, viewer ID, and timestamp.

FR3. Profile Interaction Options  
FR3.1: The listener shall be able to follow or unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), view public playlists (UC-2), view song details (UC-1), return to the previous screen, or return to the home screen.  
FR3.2: If the listener is a Premium Listener viewing their own profile, they shall have the ability to update their profile details (UC-37).

FR4. Error Handling  
FR4.1: If the profile is deleted, private, or otherwise unavailable, the system shall display an error message and allow navigation back or to the home screen.

**FR for UC-4: Listen to Song**  
FR1. Song Playback Controls  
FR1.1: When a listener selects a song, the client shall initiate a playback request using either streaming or local playback if the song has been downloaded.  
FR1.2: The playback interface shall provide options to play or pause the song, seek forward or backward, toggle repeat and shuffle, adjust volume, and view playback progress.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: The client shall log a song playback event that includes the song ID, listener ID, timestamp, and source context.

FR3. Contextual Navigation  
FR3.1: The playback interface shall allow the listener to access the song’s detail view (UC-1), associated playlist (UC-2), and artist or profile details (UC-3).

FR4. Premium Listener Features  
FR4.1: Premium Listeners shall be able to add or remove the song to or from their own playlists (UC-28/UC-29) and download the song or remove the download (UC-24/UC-25).

FR5. Error Handling  
FR5.1: If the song cannot be played due to availability issues, restrictions, or removal, the system shall notify the listener with an appropriate error message.

**FR for UC-5: Listen to Playlist**  
FR1. Playlist Playback Functionality  
FR1.1: When a listener initiates playback for a playlist, the client shall begin playing songs in sequence or shuffle mode depending on the user's settings.  
FR1.2: The playback screen shall include controls to pause or resume playback, skip tracks, seek within a track, toggle repeat for track or playlist, adjust volume, and view or rearrange the queue.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: Each song played as part of a playlist playback session shall be recorded as an individual song play event with appropriate metadata.

FR3. Navigation and Interaction  
FR3.1: The listener shall be able to access the playlist detail view (UC-2), song detail view (UC-1), or add a song to favourites (UC-6).

FR4. Premium Listener Capabilities  
FR4.1: Premium Listeners may add or remove songs to or from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) and download songs for offline playback (UC-24/UC-25).

FR5. Error Handling  
FR5.1: If the playlist or its contents are unavailable, the system shall display a message stating that the playlist is unavailable and offer the option to return.

**FR for UC-6: Add Song to Favourites**  
FR1. Favouriting Action  
FR1.1: When a listener taps the favourite icon for a song, the client shall send a request to the server to mark the song as a favourite for the current user.  
FR1.2: Upon success, the song shall be added to the listener’s favourites list, the global favourite count shall be incremented, and the UI shall update to reflect the new status with feedback such as a toast message or animation.

FR2. Error Handling  
FR2.1: If the action fails due to unavailability or network issues, the client shall notify the listener and prevent state change until the action can be retried.

### 

**FR for UC-7: Remove Song from Favourites**  
FR1. Unfavouriting Action  
FR1.1: When the listener taps the favourite icon on a previously favourited song, the client shall send a request to the server to remove it from the user’s favourites list.  
FR1.2: Upon confirmation, the favourite count shall be decremented, and the UI shall reflect the change by toggling the icon and showing a confirmation message.

FR2. Error Handling  
FR2.1: If the unfavouriting action fails due to network or server error, the system shall notify the listener and preserve the original state until retry.

**FR for UC-8: Add Playlist to Favourites**  
FR1. Playlist Favouriting Action  
FR1.1: When the listener selects “Add to Favourites” from a playlist view, the client shall send a request to the server to add the playlist to the listener’s favourites list.  
FR1.2: Upon success, the playlist shall be added to the favourites section and the interface shall be updated to reflect the favourited state.  
FR1.3: A confirmation message or visual cue shall be shown.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A favourite playlist event shall be logged with playlist ID, listener ID, and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist is no longer available or made private, the system shall display an error message and cancel the action.

**FR for UC-9: Remove Playlist from Favourites**  
FR1. Unfavouriting a Playlist  
FR1.1: When the listener chooses to remove a playlist from favourites, the client shall send a request to the server to update the listener’s library accordingly.  
FR1.2: Upon success, the playlist shall be removed from the favourites list, and the UI shall reflect the change immediately with feedback such as an updated icon or confirmation message.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A remove favourite playlist event shall be sent to the analytics system.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist is unavailable or the session has expired, the system shall display an error message and prevent the change.

**FR for UC-10: Add Profile to Favourites**  
FR1. Profile Favouriting Action  
FR1.1: When a listener selects the favourite or follow button on a public profile, the client shall send a request to the server to mark the profile as favourited for the user.  
FR1.2: Upon confirmation, the profile shall appear in the listener’s favourites list and the interface shall update to reflect the new status.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A profile favouriting event shall be recorded with profile ID and user ID.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the profile is private or deleted, the system shall prevent the action and notify the user.

### 

**FR for UC-11: Remove Profile from Favourites**  
FR1. Profile Unfavouriting Action  
FR1.1: When a listener selects the unfollow or unfavourite button for a profile, the system shall remove the profile from the listener’s favourites list.  
FR1.2: The UI shall update to reflect the change, and the system shall confirm the operation visually.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: An unfavourite profile event shall be recorded in the analytics system.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the profile has already been removed or is unavailable, the system shall show a message and cancel the operation.

**FR for UC-12: Search Songs**  
FR1. Song Search Functionality  
FR1.1: When the listener enters a query in the search interface and selects the “Songs” category, the client shall send the search term to the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall respond with a list of songs that match the query, sorted by relevance. Each result shall include the title, artist name, duration (in seconds), and album name.  
FR1.3: The client shall display the results in a scrollable list with appropriate formatting.  
FR1.4: The listener shall be able to view song details (UC-1), play the song (UC-4), or add/remove the song to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7).  
FR1.5: Premium Listeners shall also be able to add or remove the song to or from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) and download the song (UC-24).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A search event shall be recorded, including the search query, timestamp, and user identifier.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If no results are found, the system shall display a “No songs found” message and suggest alternative queries.

**FR for UC-13: Search Playlists**  
FR1. Playlist Search Functionality  
FR1.1: When the listener selects the “Playlists” tab in the search view, the client shall send the search query to the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall return a list of public playlists, each including the playlist title, owner name, and total song count.  
FR1.3: The client shall display playlists with basic metadata in a scrollable format.  
FR1.4: Listeners may view playlist details (UC-2), play the playlist (UC-5), or add/remove it from favourites (UC-8/UC-9).  
FR1.5: If the playlist belongs to the Premium Listener, additional actions shall be supported such as editing or deleting the playlist (UC-29/UC-30) and setting the playlist access level (UC-31).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist search event shall be sent to the analytics system.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If no playlists are found, the system shall display a “No playlists found” message and suggest exploring trending playlists (UC-16).

**FR for UC-14: Search Profiles**  
FR1. Profile Search Functionality  
FR1.1: When the listener searches using the “Profiles” category, the client shall send the query to the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall return a list of matching public profiles including profile name and profile picture URL.  
FR1.3: The client shall display the results in a scrollable list.  
FR1.4: The listener shall be able to view profile details (UC-3), follow or unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), and explore the user’s public playlists (UC-2).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A profile search event shall be recorded with the query, user ID, and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If no profiles are found, the system shall show a “No profiles found” message and recommend trending profiles (UC-17/UC-20).

**FR for UC-15: View Trending Songs**  
FR1. Trending Songs Display  
FR1.1: Upon accessing the home screen, the client shall request trending song data from the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall return a ranked list of trending songs, each with title, artist name, album name, and duration.  
FR1.3: The listener may view song details (UC-1), play the song (UC-4), or add/remove the song to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7).  
FR1.4: Premium Listeners shall also be able to add or remove the song to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) and download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A “view trending songs” event shall be recorded when the section is viewed.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If trending data is unavailable, the system shall display an error message with options to retry or return to the home screen.

**FR for UC-16: View Trending Playlists**  
FR1. Trending Playlist Display  
FR1.1: When the listener accesses the trending section, the client shall request a curated list of popular playlists from the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall return playlist summaries including the title, owner, and song count.  
FR1.3: The listener may view playlist details (UC-2), play the playlist (UC-5), and add or remove the playlist to/from favourites (UC-8/UC-9).  
FR1.4: If the listener owns the playlist and is a Premium Listener, they may edit the playlist (UC-29) and download the playlist (UC-26/UC-27).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A trending playlist view event shall be recorded.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If data retrieval fails, the system shall display “Unable to load trending playlists” with fallback actions.

### 

**FR for UC-17: View Trending Profiles**  
FR1. Trending Profiles Display  
FR1.1: When accessing the trending section, the client shall request trending profiles from the analytics system.  
FR1.2: Each returned profile shall include the profile name, profile picture URL, and popularity metadata.  
FR1.3: The listener may view the profile (UC-3), follow or unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), and access songs or playlists related to the profile (UC-1/UC-2).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A trending profile view event shall be recorded.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the data is unavailable, the system shall show an error message and allow the listener to retry or return to the home screen.

**FR for UC-18: View Recommended Songs**  
FR1. Recommended Songs Retrieval and Display  
FR1.1: Upon visiting the home screen, the client shall request personalized song recommendations using the listener’s identifier.  
FR1.2: The server shall return a list of songs curated based on listening history, favourite songs, and behavior of similar users.  
FR1.3: The songs shall be presented in a scrollable list or carousel.  
FR1.4: The listener may view song details (UC-1), play the song (UC-4), add or remove the song to/from favourites (UC-6/UC-7), and view the artist’s profile (UC-3).  
FR1.5: Premium Listeners may add or remove songs to/from playlists they own (UC-28/UC-29) and download the song (UC-24/UC-25).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A recommended song view event shall be recorded per session.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If recommendations are not available, the system shall show a fallback message or display trending content (UC-15).

**FR for UC-19: View Recommended Playlists**  
FR1. Personalized Playlist Recommendations  
FR1.1: When the home screen loads, the client shall request recommended playlists from the recommendation engine.  
FR1.2: The backend shall return curated playlists based on favourite songs, listener behavior, and popular playlists among similar users.  
FR1.3: The listener may view playlist details (UC-2), play the playlist (UC-5), add or remove the playlist to/from favourites (UC-8/UC-9), and view the playlist owner’s profile (UC-3).  
FR1.4: Premium Listeners may add or remove songs from these playlists to their own (UC-28/UC-29) and download the playlist (UC-26).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A recommended playlist view event shall be recorded when the section is accessed.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If no recommended playlists are available, the system shall display “No playlist recommendations yet” and encourage more listening activity.

**FR for UC-20: View Recommended Profiles**  
FR1. Profile Recommendation Display  
FR1.1: When the listener accesses the home screen, the client shall request recommended profiles based on the listener’s data.  
FR1.2: The server shall return profiles selected using engagement history, genre preferences, and follow patterns of similar users.  
FR1.3: The listener may view the profile (UC-3), follow or unfollow the profile (UC-10/UC-11), and access songs or playlists associated with the profile (UC-1/UC-2).  
FR1.4: Premium Listeners may add songs to their playlists (UC-28) and download songs (UC-24).

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A profile recommendation view event shall be logged when the section loads.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If no personalized profiles are available, the system shall show trending profiles (UC-17) instead.

**FR for UC-21: Share Song**  
FR1. Sharing Song Functionality  
FR1.1: When a listener selects the share option for a song, the client shall request a shareable link from the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall generate and return a unique URL that links to the song’s detail view.  
FR1.3: The client shall present options to copy the link or share it through supported third-party platforms such as messaging apps and social media.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A song share event shall be recorded with the song identifier, listener identifier, and chosen sharing method (if applicable).

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the song is deleted or unavailable, the client shall display the message “This song cannot be shared at this time” and offer the listener the ability to return to the previous or home screen.

**FR for UC-22: Share Playlist**  
FR1. Playlist Sharing Functionality  
FR1.1: When a listener selects the share option from the playlist details view, the client shall request a shareable link from the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall generate and return a unique URL referencing the playlist's details.  
FR1.3: The client shall provide the listener with options to either copy the link or share it directly via supported third-party platforms such as messaging apps or social media.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist share event shall be recorded by the analytics system, including the playlist identifier, listener identifier, and the chosen share method if available.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist is private, deleted, or otherwise unavailable, the system shall display the message “This playlist cannot be shared” and provide options to return to the previous screen or homescreen.

**FR for UC-23: Share Profile**  
FR1. Profile Sharing Functionality  
FR1.1: When a listener selects the share option from a profile view, the client shall request a shareable link to the profile from the server.  
FR1.2: The server shall generate a unique URL pointing to the profile view and return it to the client.  
FR1.3: The client shall present the user with options to copy the link or share it using supported third-party platforms such as email, social media, or messaging apps.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A profile share event shall be recorded with the profile identifier, sharer ID, and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the profile is deleted, private, or inaccessible, the system shall notify the listener with the message “This profile cannot be shared at this time” and offer navigation options.

**FR for UC-24: Add Song to Downloads**  
FR1. Song Download Process  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener taps the download button for a song, the client shall initiate a request to download the corresponding audio file.  
FR1.2: The system shall validate that the user has Premium status, the song exists, and that it is available for download.  
FR1.3: Upon successful validation, the audio file shall be downloaded and stored locally on the device.  
FR1.4: The downloaded song shall appear in the listener’s downloads section for offline playback.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A song download event shall be recorded with the song ID, user ID, and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the download cannot be completed due to connectivity issues, storage limitations, or licensing restrictions, the system shall display an appropriate error message and suggest retrying.

**FR for UC-25: Remove Song from Downloads**  
FR1. Song Removal Process  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener selects the remove option for a previously downloaded song, the system shall delete the local copy of the song from the device’s storage.  
FR1.2: The client shall update the downloads list to reflect the change, and the song shall no longer be accessible offline.  
FR1.3: The song shall remain available for online streaming unless it is removed from the catalog.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A song removal from downloads event shall be recorded with song ID and user ID.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the removal fails due to file system issues or the file no longer existing, the system shall show an appropriate error message and allow retrying or refreshing the downloads view.

**FR for UC-26: Add Playlist to Downloads**  
FR1. Playlist Download Process  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener selects the download option for a playlist, the client shall initiate a request to download the playlist metadata and its songs.  
FR1.2: The system shall confirm that the playlist is public or owned by the user and that the songs are eligible for download.  
FR1.3: The client shall download the playlist metadata and begin downloading each song in the playlist individually.  
FR1.4: Upon completion, the playlist shall be listed in the listener’s Playlist Downloads, and the songs shall also appear in the Song Downloads section.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist download event shall be recorded, along with individual song download events for each successfully downloaded track.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If some or all downloads fail due to connectivity, storage, or content restrictions, the system shall notify the user and indicate which items failed, while preserving any successful downloads.

**FR for UC-27: Remove Playlist from Downloads**  
FR1. Playlist Removal Process  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener selects the remove option for a previously downloaded playlist, the system shall delete the playlist metadata from the local Playlist Downloads section.  
FR1.2: The system shall not remove any individual songs that were downloaded as part of the playlist unless the listener chooses to remove them separately.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist removal from downloads event shall be recorded with the playlist ID and user ID.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist cannot be removed due to file system errors or a sync issue, the system shall display an error message and offer the listener the option to retry.

**FR for UC-28: Create Playlist**  
FR1. Playlist Creation Workflow  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener selects the “Create Playlist” option, the system shall prompt for a playlist title and optionally a description and access level.  
FR1.2: Upon submission, the system shall validate the inputs and create a new playlist object in the database, assigning ownership to the listener.  
FR1.3: The newly created playlist shall appear in the listener’s profile and library and shall be ready for further editing or song addition.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist creation event shall be logged with the user ID and playlist ID.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the title is empty or invalid, the system shall display a validation error.  
FR3.2: If the playlist creation fails due to connectivity or server error, the system shall notify the user and allow them to retry.

**FR for UC-29: Update Playlist**  
FR1. Playlist Editing Functionality  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener opens one of their playlists and chooses to edit, the system shall allow them to update the title, description, song list, and song order.  
FR1.2: The client shall submit all changes to the server in a single update request.  
FR1.3: The server shall validate ownership and update the playlist accordingly in the system.  
FR1.4: Upon success, the updated playlist shall be reflected across all views.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist update event shall be recorded with relevant metadata.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist no longer exists or the user is not the owner, the system shall return an access error.  
FR3.2: If the update fails due to a system error, the listener shall be notified and may retry.

**FR for UC-30: Delete Playlist**  
FR1. Playlist Deletion Flow  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener chooses to delete one of their playlists, the system shall prompt for confirmation before proceeding.  
FR1.2: Upon confirmation, the server shall validate ownership and remove the playlist from the system.  
FR1.3: The deleted playlist shall no longer be visible in the listener’s profile or library, and references to it shall be removed from all relevant analytics and metadata.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist deletion event shall be recorded including the playlist ID and user ID.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist does not exist or deletion fails due to a backend error, the system shall inform the listener and allow retrying later.

**FR for UC-31: Set Playlist Access Level**  
FR1. Access Level Modification  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener chooses to edit the access level of one of their playlists, the system shall allow selecting between Public and Private visibility modes.  
FR1.2: Upon submission, the new access level shall be updated in the playlist metadata and saved to the system.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A playlist access level update event shall be recorded, indicating the change and affected playlist.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the playlist no longer exists or the listener is not the owner, the system shall display an error and cancel the operation.  
FR3.2: If the update fails due to a system error, the listener shall be notified and given an option to retry.

**FR for UC-32: Sign Up**  
FR1. Account Creation Process  
FR1.1: When a Guest selects the “Sign Up” option, the system shall present a registration form requesting mandatory fields including email, password, and display name.  
FR1.2: Upon form submission, the system shall validate the input for uniqueness and correctness.  
FR1.3: If the information is valid, the system shall create a new account and sign the user in.  
FR1.4: The user shall be redirected to the onboarding flow or home screen, and their account shall be classified as either Basic or Premium depending on their subscription selection.

FR2. Third-Party Registration  
FR2.1: If the Guest chooses a third-party provider (such as Google or Apple), the system shall redirect to the provider’s authentication page and process credentials.  
FR2.2: On successful verification, a new account shall be created, and the user shall be signed in automatically.

FR3. Analytics Logging  
FR3.1: A sign-up event shall be recorded with the user’s ID and method of registration.

FR4. Error Handling  
FR4.1: If submitted data is invalid or the email is already in use, the system shall highlight the error and prompt for correction.

**FR for UC-33: Sign In**  
FR1. Sign-In Functionality  
FR1.1: When a Guest selects “Sign In,” the system shall present a login form with fields for email or username and password.  
FR1.2: Upon form submission, the system shall authenticate the credentials and, if correct, grant access and sign the user in.  
FR1.3: The user shall be redirected to the home screen or the last known session state.

FR2. Third-Party Sign-In  
FR2.1: If the user selects a third-party login option, the system shall verify credentials through the provider and, upon success, log the user in.

FR3. Analytics Logging  
FR3.1: A sign-in event shall be recorded with the user ID and method of authentication.

FR4. Error Handling  
FR4.1: If authentication fails, the system shall display an error and prompt the user to retry.

**FR for UC-34: Sign Out**  
FR1. Sign-Out Process  
FR1.1: When a signed-in user selects “Sign Out,” the system shall terminate the session and clear all locally stored session data.  
FR1.2: The user shall be redirected to the welcome or login screen.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A sign-out event shall be recorded including the user ID and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If there is a network or server issue during sign-out, the system shall still clear the local session and notify the user that remote logout may not be synchronized.

**FR for UC-35: Recover Account**  
FR1. Recovery via Email  
FR1.1: When a Guest selects “Forgot Password,” the system shall prompt for the registered email or username.  
FR1.2: Upon validation, the system shall send a secure recovery link or reset code to the user’s email.

FR2. Third-Party Recovery  
FR2.1: If the account is linked to a third-party service, the user may initiate recovery via provider login.  
FR2.2: Upon successful authentication, access to the account shall be restored.

FR3. Analytics Logging  
FR3.1: A recovery request event shall be logged with the provided identifier and request time.

FR4. Error Handling  
FR4.1: If the submitted email or username does not match an account, the system shall display an appropriate message and allow retry.

**FR for UC-36: Delete Account**  
FR1. Account Deletion Workflow  
FR1.1: When a signed-in listener selects the “Delete Account” option, the system shall prompt for confirmation through password entry or code verification.  
FR1.2: Upon confirmation, the system shall permanently delete the listener’s account and all associated data, including playlists, favourites, and history.  
FR1.3: The user shall be logged out and redirected to the welcome screen.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A deletion event shall be recorded with user ID and deletion timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If confirmation fails or the user cancels the deletion process, no data shall be deleted.  
FR3.2: If a server error occurs, the user shall be notified, and the system shall allow retrying later.

**FR for UC-37: Adjust Account Settings**  
FR1. Settings Modification Flow  
FR1.1: When a listener accesses account settings, the system shall allow modifications to display name, profile picture, email, password, and notification preferences.  
FR1.2: Upon submission, the system shall validate the changes and apply them to the user’s profile.  
FR1.3: Sensitive fields such as email or password changes may require re-authentication before changes are confirmed.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: An account settings update event shall be recorded, noting which fields were changed.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the submitted data is invalid or conflicts with existing records, the system shall display an error and prompt correction.

**FR for UC-38: View Subscription Details**  
FR1. Subscription Information Retrieval  
FR1.1: When a signed-in listener navigates to the subscription section, the system shall retrieve and display the current subscription status.  
FR1.2: The information displayed shall include subscription type (Basic or Premium), status (active or inactive), renewal or expiration date, billing method, and benefits summary.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A subscription detail view event shall be recorded with the user ID and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If subscription data is unavailable, the system shall show an appropriate error message and suggest contacting support.

**FR for UC-39: Activate Subscription**  
FR1. Subscription Activation Workflow  
FR1.1: When a Basic Listener selects “Upgrade to Premium,” the system shall display available Premium plans and benefits.  
FR1.2: Upon selecting a plan and submitting valid payment details, the system shall process the payment and activate Premium access.  
FR1.3: The listener’s account shall be immediately upgraded and reflect Premium status.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A subscription activation event shall be logged including user ID, selected plan, and timestamp.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If payment fails, the system shall notify the user and prompt for an alternate payment method.  
FR3.2: If the user already has an active Premium subscription, the system shall prevent redundant activation and display an informational message.

**FR for UC-40: Cancel Subscription**  
FR1. Subscription Cancellation Process  
FR1.1: When a Premium Listener chooses to cancel their subscription, the system shall present a confirmation screen detailing the impact of cancellation.  
FR1.2: Upon confirmation, the system shall mark the subscription for cancellation at the end of the billing cycle.  
FR1.3: The listener shall retain Premium access until the cycle ends, after which the account shall revert to Basic status.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A cancellation event shall be recorded with user ID and scheduled expiration date.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If cancellation fails due to a server issue, the system shall display an error and provide retry or support contact options.

**FR for UC-41: Renew Subscription**  
FR1. Subscription Renewal Workflow  
FR1.1: When a listener without an active Premium subscription accesses the subscription section, the system shall detect the inactive status and display available renewal options.  
FR1.2: The listener shall be able to select a Premium plan, submit payment, and upon success, the Premium subscription shall be reactivated immediately.  
FR1.3: Premium features shall be restored to the listener’s account.

FR2. Analytics Logging  
FR2.1: A renewal event shall be recorded with user ID and selected plan details.

FR3. Error Handling  
FR3.1: If the renewal fails due to payment issues, the system shall show an appropriate message and allow an alternative payment attempt.

#### 

#### 

####   

*This page intentionally left blank*

# **3\. System Architecture** {#3.-system-architecture}

---

The architecture of a system is the shape given to that system by those who build it. The form of that shape is in the division of that system into components, the arrangement of those components, and the ways in which those components communicate with each other. The purpose of that shape is to facilitate the development, deployment, operation, and maintenance of the software system contained within it. The strategy behind that facilitation is to leave as many options open as possible, for as long as possible.[^1]

4.1. Major design criteria	00  
4.2. From a bird’s eye view	00  
4.3. The Core layer	00  
4.3.1. Domain Entities	00  
4.3.2. Use Cases	00  
4.3.2.1. Boundaries	00  
4.3.2.2. Interactors	00  
4.3.2.3. Gateways	00  
4.4. The Infrastructures layer	00  
4.5. The Configurations layer	00

## **3.1. Major design criteria** {#3.1.-major-design-criteria}

Before we start with the description of the architecture we have crafted, we need to explicitly document what have been our design criteria and what are our strategic decisions.

### **Clear Separation of Concerns**

Internally, the system should be composed of maximally cohesive components that are minimally coupled to each other.[^2] Dependencies between components in the system should be unidirectional and simple to manage. The dependency arrows should always point downward, with layers depending only on the next adjacent lower layer.  
Externally, the system should be independent of frameworks, databases, UI, or any other infrastructural concerns. The system may adopt any frameworks, databases, UI, or any other infrastructure components as it desires, but the system should not be tightly coupled to any of them. The system should have the flexibility to easily swap, add, or remove infrastructures without affecting the business rules[^3]. Options should be kept open and at arm’s length[^4].

### **Maintainability and Scalability**

The system should accommodate continually changing requirements without necessitating major rewrites. Changes should be isolated to the relevant components without unexpected side effects.  
Source code should be easy to read in order to be easy to write. Reading source code should scream its intent.[^5] How existing code should be read, how new code should be added, how new code should integrate with existing code, etc. should be clear and consistent. We expect source code to be easy to delete.[^6]  
We expect independent deployability and developability. When a source code in a component changes, only that component should need to be redeployed. If the components in the system can be deployed independently, they can be developed independently by different teams.  
Performance optimizations should be accommodated, but they should not come at the cost of system maintainability and/or architectural integrity. Performance optimizations should be considered only after the system is analyzed and bottlenecks are identified. Any trivial performance optimization that does not address the actual bottlenecks or violates architectural policies should not be considered.

### **Testability in Isolation**

The system should be highly testable. The business rules should be tested without the database, UI, web server, or any other infrastructures. We expect tests to be granular[^7] and encompassing[^8]. We expect the test coverage to remain high.  
Testing should only happen at system boundaries, not internal implementations[^9] \- they should be able to freely change, unconstrained by the tests. Adding new tests should be trivial.[^10] Executing tests should not be time-consuming.

### 

### **Explicit is better than Implicit[^11]**

Components in the system should have clear, well-defined boundaries, via which interactions are facilitated. Data transformations and dependencies should be clear and explicit. The naming conventions, design patterns, and anything that contributes to the overall code style should be consistent across the system.  
Implicit, global states (e.g. Singleton) should be avoided at all costs. We certainly do not expect any hidden coupling.[^12]

## **3.2. From a bird’s eye view** {#3.2.-from-a-bird’s-eye-view}

#### 

#### **![][image3]**

#### **Figure 5.1. System Architecture, from a bird’s eye view** {#figure-5.1.-system-architecture,-from-a-bird’s-eye-view}

## **The Dependency Rule**

Observe the direction of the dependencies. All dependencies cross the boundary lines in one direction, and they always point towards the components containing the higher-level policies. This architectural integrity is known as the Dependency Rule. 

*Source code dependencies must point only inward, toward higher-level policies.*[^13]

Nothing in an inner circle can know anything at all about something in an outer circle. In particular, the name of something declared in an outer circle must not be mentioned by the code in an inner circle. That includes functions, classes. variables, or any other named software entity.  
It is the Dependency Rule that makes this architecture work.

## 

## **3.3. The Core layer** {#3.3.-the-core-layer}

The software in this layer is the heart and foundation of the system. It is self-contained and implementation-agnostic. It holds all the essential high-level business rules and policies.  
This layer follows a crucial rule \- it should be agnostic to any externalities, whether they are developed internally or externally. The only allowed dependencies are the programming language and its standard libraries[^14]. Externalities are introduced via abstractions. This layer should house abstractions only; never any implementations. One exception to this rule applies to tools that generate code at compile-time, such as Project Lombok[^15]. They do not introduce runtime dependencies and if desired, they can be easily removed by substituting annotations with generated codes.  
As dictated by the Dependency Rule, changes should only propagate from the inner layers to the outer layers, not the other way around. In other words, changes in any other layers should never trigger a change in the Core layer.  
The Core layer is divided into two sub-layers separated by an architectural boundary: the Domain Entities and the Use Cases.

### **3.3.1. Domain Entities** {#3.3.1.-domain-entities}

Domain Entities are higher-level business rules that are system-agnostic.  
A Domain Entity is a single and separate software module that binds together the Critical Business Rules[^16] and the Critical Business Data[^17]. It either contains the Critical Business Data or has very easy access to that data. Its interface consists of the functions that implement the Critical Business Rules that operate on that data.  
The software in this sub-layer stands alone as representative of the business. It is pure business and nothing else. It is agnostic of any automated system, and it is agnostic of any concern in any system outside the business rules. It could serve the business in any system regardless of how that system was persisted, or presented, or deployed, or how it was used by that system.  
We expect the Domain Entities to be completely invisible to the outside layers. Not anything but the Interactors and Gateways should know about them.

### **3.3.2. Use Cases** {#3.3.2.-use-cases}

Use Cases are lower-level business rules that are system-specific.  
The software in this sub-layer encapsulates and implements all the use cases of the system. A use case, at a high level, is a description of the way that an automated system is used. It specifies the input to be provided by the user, the output to be returned to the user, and the processing steps involved in producing that output.[^18] Unlike Domain-Driven Design, it does not differentiate between Commands, Queries, Assertions, and Side-effect-free Functions.[^19]  
The Dependency Rule still applies. A use case does not describe the user interface other than to informally specify the data coming in from and going back through that interface. How that data gets in and out of the system is irrelevant to the use case. We do, however, expect that changes to the business rules of the system will affect the use cases and therefore the software in this sub-layer. If the details of a use case change, then some software in this sub-layer will certainly be affected.  
The Use Cases sub-layer is divided into three components: the Boundaries, the Interactors, and the Gateways.

#### **3.3.2.1. Boundaries** {#3.3.2.1.-boundaries}

The Boundaries define the interfaces through which the outside world interacts with the use cases of the system. They serve as the entry points to the core business logic and decouple external concerns from internal behaviour.  
The data that cross the Boundaries are pure Data Transfer Objects. They are simple, stateless, immutable data structures designed exclusively for data interchange. They do not contain any business logic and therefore do not reference the Domain Entities. They are also agnostic of any transport protocols or delivery mechanisms. Using a Data Access Object of a particular DBMS as the data model for the Boundaries, for example, would cause external implementation details to leak into the Boundaries and therefore, violate our architectural integrity.

#### **3.3.2.2. Interactors** {#3.3.2.2.-interactors}

The Interactors implement the use cases of the system. They contain rich system-specific business logic. They orchestrate the dance of the Domain Entities and delegate infrastructural concerns via the Gateways. Inside an Interactor we expect a high-level, pseudo-code-like description of, or fulfilling, a particular business rule. Such description remains idempotent regardless of transport protocols, delivery mechanisms, or runtime environments used by the system. For example, whether they are running inside a GUI application, a system-level daemon, or a web server should be irrelevant to the Interactors.  
In alignment with the Single Responsibility Principle, an Interactor should only do one thing, and one thing only. That means it must not reference other Interactors \- promoting code reusability introduces tighter coupling. Each Interactor is therefore a complete and cohesive unit of behaviour.

#### **3.3.2.3. Gateways** {#3.3.2.3.-gateways}

The Gateways are abstractions that reflect the system’s infrastructural requirements. They can be aligned with the concepts of Ports in Hexagonal (Ports and Adapters) Architecture.  
They define the interface through which the Interactors interact with externalities, often infrastructural, such as persistence mechanisms, network services, encryption utilities, or any other infrastructure that supports the Interactors’ execution. Gateways should be expressed in the language of the business domain i.e. Ubiquitous Language in Domain-Driven Design, rather than exposing the details of infrastructural externalities. They should abstract over the infrastructural requirements for some particular component, not mirroring its full capabilities.  
Changes in infrastructural components, for example switching from a MySQL database to a NoSQL database, does not involve modifying the Gateways. The Core layer is therefore shielded from such changes.

## **3.4. The Infrastructures layer** {#3.4.-the-infrastructures-layer}

The software in this layer is a set of adapters that convert data from the format most convenient for the use cases and domain entities, to the format most convenient for external agencies such as the Database or the Web.  
The Infrastructures layer is divided into 2 parts: the Gateways and the Interface Adapters. We then organize each part into sub-components based on different overarching technologies. We surely do not expect changes in an infrastructural sub-component to affect any others.  
When any of the external parts of the application become obsolete, we can replace those obsolete elements with a minimum of fuss, without modifying the Core layer.

### **3.4.1. Interface Adapters** {#3.4.1.-interface-adapters}

The Interface Adapters serve as the bridge between external interfaces, typically associated with presentation or communication. They make use of the Core’s Boundaries to access the use cases of the application. It is the Interface Adapters, for example, that will wholly contain the MVC “architecture” of a GUI. The Presenters, Views, and Controllers all belong here. The models are likely just data structures that are passed from the Controllers to the Boundaries, and then back from the Boundaries to the Presenters and Views. Similarly, for an API-driven application, the Interface Adapters handle request parsing, authentication, and serialization before passing data to the Boundaries. The response is then formatted into any required protocol before being returned to the client.

### **3.4.2. Gateways** {#3.4.2.-gateways}

The Infrastructures’ Gateways are concrete implementations of the Core’s Gateways, and can be aligned with the concepts of Adapters in Hexagonal Architecture. They fulfill the Core’s infrastructural requirements by bridging business logic with external systems such as the Database, the API, and system services. Each Gateway is responsible for one specific, non-overlapping infrastructural concern.  
As per the Dependency Rule, the Gateways do not know about each other, or whether they will be used, or how they might be used. When the application’s infrastructural requirements change, we expect some Gateways in the Core layer to change, and so are the implementing Gateways in the Infrastructures layer. But never the other way around.

## **3.5. The Configurations layer** {#3.5.-the-configurations-layer}

The software in this layer is the “glue” that connects and wires the different layers of the application. It involves making decisions about the infrastructures to use, selecting the relevant use cases, determining which endpoints to expose, and choosing the appropriate technologies.  
The Configurations layer houses one component that creates, coordinates, and oversees the others. We call this the Main component.[^20]  
The Main component is the ultimate detail \- the lowest-level policy. It is the initial entry point of the system, and operates behind an architectural boundary. We think of it as a plugin[^21] to the application \- a centralized component that sets up the initial conditions and configurations, gathers all the outside resources, and then hands control over to the high-level policies of the application. It is in this Main component that dependencies should be injected, either manually or preferably via a Dependency Injection[^22] framework. It is possible to have multiple Main components, the same way that a system can have multiple plugins.  
In practice, multiple Main components should coexist within the system, each tailored to a specific, non-overlapping configuration. We expect the Configurations layer to be organized as a collection of configurations that is independent and independently deployable. We expect, for example, separate Main components for differing environments, or presentational technologies, or deployment platforms.

# **4\. System Detailed Design** {#4.-system-detailed-design}

---

## **4.1. Class diagrams** {#4.1.-class-diagrams}

### **4.1.1. The Domain Entities** {#4.1.1.-the-domain-entities}

The following diagram illustrates how we choose to represent the Domain Entities. At a high level, using Domain-Driven Design terminologies, the User entity serves as a common superclass, with Listener and Artist inheriting from it to represent distinct user roles. Each entity is composed 1:1 with its respective value objects e.g., a Song has a SongMetadata. An interesting approach to reduce coupling and maintain separation of concerns is that entities store references to related domain objects as identifiers (e.g., artistIds, playlistIds) rather than as full object instances. This design choice is reflected in the diagram using dotted arrows instead of aggregations, indicating knowledge of external entities without direct ownership or structural inclusion.

### **![][image4]**

The following diagram illustrates the infrastructural requirements of the system’s use cases.

## **5.1. Desktop Version** {#5.1.-desktop-version}

Figma design public link: [Curtain-Desktop](https://www.figma.com/design/EYIrOIhSMa59vz4fiDu9vW/Curtain-Desktop?node-id=0-1&t=6N8jrmjWjFbG5a4D-1)

- We decided to choose Figma for our UI designs of Curtain. The main reason for this is Figma’s flexibility, simplicity and wide variety of features. Figma supports auto layout which makes the UI pages responsive, a large library for icons and fonts, and many more. It also allows us to create components to make the design process more efficient and streamlined.   
- The link above provides you with an interactive and comprehensive view of the desktop version. Below this is a short demonstration.

# **6\. Appendices** ---

 {#6.-appendices}

## **6.1. Appendix A** {#6.1.-appendix-a}

**System Architecture:**  
**A Comparative Study**

### **A.1. 	[Model-View-Controller Pattern and derivatives](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/MVC)** {#a.1.-model-view-controller-pattern-and-derivatives}

The Model-View-Controller (MVC) and its derivatives such as Model-View-Presenter (MVP), Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM), Model-View-Presenter-Controller (MVPC) have been a dominant paradigm in UI development. Adopting our architecture does not exclude the use of separated presentation patterns such as MVC. Using MVC or any of its derivatives as the overarching architecture, however, is not suitable for our application.  
The classic MVC has a Controller that processes user input, updates the Model, and refreshes the View. In many implementations, however, we observed that the Controller is often tasked with additional responsibilities, leading to the Fat Controller problem \- a clear violation of the Single Responsibility Principle. Other derivatives try to solve this by introducing a Presenter (MVP) or a ViewModel (MVVM). We eliminate this by separating the business logic into Domain Entities, Boundaries, and Interactors, each with a clear, deterministic, and non-overlapping responsibility. Additionally, we do not enforce a rigid view-binding mechanism or tie our application to the UI framework at the business logic level. We allow different presentation mechanisms to interact with the same underlying business logic via standardized Interface Adapters.  
One of the fundamental drawbacks of MVC “architectures” is the tendency for business logic to “leak” into Controllers or Presenters. This is inevitable, since MVC architectures primarily focus on structuring the presentation rather than establishing a robust domain model. In our architecture, Controllers and Presenters contain no decision-making logic, only parsing logic.  
Dependency management also becomes an issue. MVC architectures often struggle with dependency management due to their implicit reliance on framework-specific constructs. Decisions have to be made early. Substitution mid-developing is almost impossible. That never happens in our abstraction-rich architecture.  
MVC “architecture” and its derivatives are predominantly UI-centric and are better suited for frontend-heavy applications that contain little business logic. They struggle with Separation of Concerns at scale and often require significant rework when extending the system beyond the preselected UI framework. We, however, require a more robust foundation for a multi-interface, domain-centric system.

### 

### **A.2. 	[Hexagonal (Ports and Adapters) Architecture](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/cloud-design-patterns/hexagonal-architecture.html#:~:text=The%20hexagonal%20architecture%20pattern%2C%20which,or%20user%20interfaces%20\(UIs\).)** {#a.2.-hexagonal-(ports-and-adapters)-architecture}

Both our architecture and Hexagonal Architecture promote Separation of Concerns at the business logic level by prioritizing business logic isolation. Both enforces inward dependencies \- externalities uni-directionally depend on the business logic, never the other way around.  
One key difference is the way abstractions are formed. Hexagonal Architecture uses generic Ports with great flexibility in infrastructure choices. Our architecture, in contrast, uses use-case driven Gateways that are modelled around the infrastructural requirements of the business, rather than just generic abstractions. We ensure that external dependencies are tailored to actual use cases, reducing unnecessary abstraction overhead.

### **A.3. 	[Domain-Driven Design](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/DomainDrivenDesign.html)** {#a.3.-domain-driven-design}

The key difference is our architecture is use-case driven rather than domain-driven. DDD revolves around modeling complex business domains, emphasizing ubiquitous language, aggregates, and bounded contexts to ensure domain logic remains pure and expressive. Our architecture structures interactions around Interactors instead, treating every business logic execution based on I/O DTOs, rather than centering everything around domain models.  
DDD uses Aggregates and Entities to encapsulate domain behaviour, enforcing consistency through invariants. Our architecture separates lower-level behaviour from high-level policies by placing autonomous business logic inside Interactors while keeping the Domain Entities primarily data-focused. We make our Boundaries and Interactors explicit. In DDD, behaviour is often implicit within domain models.  
Repositories in DDD, similar to Hexagonal Architecture’s Ports, can be too generic and therefore over-abstracting. In DDD, there might often be the one-size-fits-all interface for persistence.  
DDD excels in complex domains where deep business rules, policies, and relationships exist. However, its use of Aggregates, Bounded Contexts, and Repositories can introduce overhead and unnecessary complexity. We realize that our application needs less of such strong modeling capabilities in the domain, and more of explicit and simplified DTO-driven interactions. Therefore we adopt a barebone version of DDD.

### **A.6. 	[Clean Architecture](https://blog.cleancoder.com/uncle-bob/2012/08/13/the-clean-architecture.html)** {#a.6.-clean-architecture}

Any attentive reader can point out the many similarities between our architecture and Uncle Bob’s Clean Architecture. Indeed, our architecture was heavily influenced by Clean Architecture itself. We agree with most of the things mentioned in the book (ref here). We could even name this chapter “Our Interpretation on Clean Architecture”\!  
[Clean Architecture is a work of utmost importance \- it recovers somewhat forgotten concepts, rules, and patterns; it clarifies useful and important concepts, rules and patterns; it tells us how all these concepts, rules and patterns fit together to provide us with a standardized way to build complex applications with maintainability in mind.](https://herbertograca.com/2017/09/28/clean-architecture-standing-on-the-shoulders-of-giants/) It introduced ideas that are foundational to our architecture. However, so as Simon Brown, we firmly believe that “the devil is in the implementation details \[...\] it’s really easy to fall at the last hurdle if you don’t give that some thought, too”. Therefore our architecture enforces several implementation constraints. The key notions of Clean Architecture are still present, while the way code should be added or modified is more governed.  
There is also a part in the diagram introduced in the Clean Architecture blog post where we feel some friction and choose to do differently \- the usage of the Controllers, Presenters, and I/O Boundaries. We see more benefits in having the Interactors simply receiving and returning DTOs than injecting an object that gets populated with data. We prefer each component not to know too much.

*This page is intentionally left blank*

[^16]:  Business rules that are critical to the business itself and would exist even if there were no system to automate them. Robert C. Martin, *Clean Architecture: A Craftsman’s Guide to Software Structure and Design* (p. 190, Ch. 20), Prentice Hall

[^17]:  The data that would exist even if the system were not automated. Robert C. Martin, *Clean Architecture: A Craftsman’s Guide to Software Structure and Design* (p. 190, Ch. 20), Prentice Hall
